    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Wait \u2014 I want to make sure I understand before changing this. \"Gravity applied in all directions\" could mean two different things:\n\n**Option A: Radial gravity (pull outward from center).** Each cell gets a velocity bias pointing *away* from the canvas center, scaled by the Pull value. Pigment everywhere drifts outward toward whichever edge is closest. Visually: paint slowly spreads toward all four edges and drains off whichever is nearest. Like a piece of paper with a vacuum on all four sides.\n\n**Option B: Gravity in all 8 compass directions simultaneously.** Each cell gets bias = sum of all 8 compass vectors \u00d7 Pull. But the 8 compass vectors sum to (0, 0) \u2014 they cancel out. So this would be a no-op. Probably not what you mean.\n\n**Option C: The user picks the gravity direction in Open mode too.** Same as Gravity mode, but with all four edges open instead of just the ones in the direction of pull. So \"down\" pull with all 4 edges open means pigment drifts down and drains primarily off the bottom, but anything that happens to reach the sides also drains.\n\nMy guess is **A** \u2014 \"radial\" gravity, where Pull pushes outward in all directions from the center. That matches \"gravity in all directions\" most naturally and gives a distinct visual identity from gravity mode (which is unidirectional pull).\n\nWant me to do A? Or did you mean something else? If A, two follow-up questions:\n\n1. **Center of the radial field \u2014 canvas center always**, or **last-click position** (so each splash creates its own outward pull)? Canvas-center is simpler and feels more like \"the page is bulging outward\"; last-click feels more like \"every splash has its own gravity well.\"\n\n2. **Should the direction dropdown be re-enabled in Open mode for this?** Or hidden, since the direction is now \"all\"? I'd hide it (since \"radial\" doesn't fit the 8-compass dropdown) and only show the Pull slider in Open mode. Sound right?",
    "citations": []
  }
]

The combination \u2014 every cell pulling outward + open edges \u2014 makes the canvas feel like a tide receding from a center.\n\nA few things that might be fun to try with it now that it's working:\n\n**With a centered splash + radial pull**, the wave radiates AND keeps moving \u2014 instead of damping out in place like closed mode, it just keeps flowing toward the edges. That's a great long-form animation.\n\n**Off-center splash + radial pull** gives asymmetric drainage \u2014 the splash radiates from its point, but the ambient pull bends it toward the nearest edges. Looks like watercolor on slightly curved paper.\n\n**Multiple splashes with radial pull**, one near each corner, would create four converging-then-diverging waves. That might be visually striking enough to be its own portfolio piece.\n\n**Wet brush + radial pull at low strength** (Pull = 3-5) \u2014 paint a slow stroke and it'll continuously bleed outward as you draw, which could make text or shapes feel like they're being eaten by the page edges over time. Could be the basis for a really moody transition.\n\nA note on something I noticed while testing: at Pull = 10+, the radial pull is fast enough that a splash dissipates in maybe 10-15 seconds. If you want a longer-lived effect for ambient backgrounds, try Pull = 3-5. The numerical test showed 9.9% mass loss in 100 frames at Pull=10 \u2014 at Pull=3 you'd see something closer to 3% loss, which makes the wash last much longer.\n\nGlad it landed. Worth bottling that observation somewhere: *radial pull + open edges is the most successful new feature in this whole boundary-mode arc*. The directional gravity modes are physically correct but feel mechanical; radial feels alive. I'd lead with radial in any documentation or HN write-up of the edge-modes feature.",

    "text": " Yes \u2014 single edit. But let me think about what happens at Pull = 200 before changing it.\n\n**Current cap:** UI 0-100 \u2192 API 0.0-1.0 (fraction of VEL_CLAMP). At 100, the bias magnitude equals VEL_CLAMP itself, which means the bias alone is strong enough to saturate the velocity clamp on its own.\n\n**At Pull = 200:** the bias would be 2\u00d7 VEL_CLAMP. After bias is added, velocity gets clamped back down to VEL_CLAMP magnitude. So in terms of *velocity*, you can't actually go faster than VEL_CLAMP \u2014 the clamp will scale you back. But here's the interesting part: **the bias dominates more thoroughly relative to splash velocity.** A splash injects some radial outward velocity that's eventually clamped; at Pull=200, the bias arrives faster than the splash velocity can establish itself, so splashes basically just collapse into uniform outflow (for radial) or uniform downward drift (for directional).\n\nIn practice: Pull = 100 already saturates the clamp. Going higher doesn't make things move faster, it just makes the bias arrive faster and dominate other dynamics (pressure gradients, splash velocity) more aggressively.\n\n**The honest answer:** raising to 200 is fine but won't make pigment move twice as fast. It'll make the bias more decisive vs. competing forces (splash velocity, pressure dynamics). If what you're actually trying to do is make pigment move *faster* off the canvas, the right knob is the velocity clamp itself, not the bias. But raising Pull to 200 is harmless and gives you that extra \"bias dominates\" headroom for compositions where you want it.\n\nWant me to do it? Or were you noticing that Pull = 100 felt clamped, in which case raising VEL_CLAMP is the better fix?",
    "citations": []

The cross comes from CFL violation in the donor-cell advection \u2014 cells aligned with the GRID's cardinal axes (N/S/E/W in world space) over-pump when velocity\u00b7adt > 1.     "text": "**Key fact:** the comment explicitly says `VEL_CLAMP` must stay below ~1.7 for stability **with the donor-cell advection mode**. Above that, the donor-cell mode would have CFL violations (the cross artifact would return).\n\nBut: **semi-Lagrangian (the default mode) has NO CFL bound.** So with semi-Lagrangian, VEL_CLAMP can go much higher safely. With other modes (standard/clamp/substep), exceeding 1.7 would re-introduce cross artifacts.\n\nThis needs careful UI/UX. Let me think:\n\n## Options\n\n**Option A: Expose VEL_CLAMP with a generous cap (e.g., 0.5 to 10).** Warning in the tooltip that values >1.7 only work cleanly with semi-Lagrangian mode. User has to know which advection mode they're in.\n\n**Option B: Make it mode-aware.** Cap at 1.5 when advection mode is donor-cell-derived; cap at much higher when semi-Lagrangian. Slider visibly clamps as user changes advection mode. More work, fewer footguns.\n\n**Option C: Expose, but with an honest label like \"Max velocity\" and a \"may produce artifacts at high values\" note.** Trust the user.\n\nI'd lean toward **C** \u2014 you've been working with this lib long enough to know the trade-offs, and the docs already explain them. Adding magic mode-coupling would feel paternalistic.\n\n## Before I code\n\nA few clarifying questions:\n\n1. **Slider range.** Lib default is `min(1.5, 1.0/SCALE)`. Practical safe range with semi-Lagrangian (the default mode) is roughly 0.5 to 5.0 \u2014 above 5.0 the velocity field gets weird (pigment moves more than 5 cells per step, bilinear interp starts smearing). Sound right? Or do you want a wider range?\n\n2. **Default value.** Keep at lib's auto-computed default (which is SCALE-dependent), or set a fixed default like 1.5?\n\n3. **What should the slider do when user changes Resolution slider?** The Resolution slider re-computes SCALE which currently re-computes VEL_CLAMP. If the user has manually set VEL_CLAMP via the new UI, should it be overridden on resolution change or preserved? I'd lean toward \"preserved once the user has touched it\" \u2014 but it's a UX call.\n\nTell me your preferences on these three and I'll do it.",
    "citations": []
  }

Values above 0.5 feel like the page\n  // is being shaken rather than tilted.\n  gravityStrength(v) {\n    if (v !== undefined) {\n      const n = +v;\n      if (!isFinite(n)) {\n        console.warn('gravityStrength: expected number, got', v);\n        return _gravityStrength;\n      }\n      _gravityStrength = Math.max(0, n);\n      _recomputeEdgeState();\n    }\n    return _gravityStrength;\n  },\n\n  // v0.84 \u2014 Maximum per-cell velocity magnitude after the\n  // pressure/viscosity/drag update. Auto-computed from SCALE by\n  // default (so display-pixel speed is resolution-invariant), but\n  // can be overridden manually. Pass `null` to restore the\n  // auto-computed default and re-enable resolution-coupled recompute.\n  //\n  // CFL note: non-semi-Lagrangian advection modes (standard, clamp,\n  // substep) require VEL_CLAMP * (DT * 0.7) * 2 < 1, i.e.\n  // VEL_CLAMP < ~1.7. Above that, those modes will produce cardinal\n  // cross artifacts again. Semi-Lagrangian (the default) has no CFL\n  // bound, so high VEL_CLAMP values are safe in that mode. The lib\n  // does NOT enforce the bound \u2014 the caller can pass any value and\n  // see what happens, which is useful for stylistic exploration.\n  velocityClamp(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return VEL_CLAMP;\n    if (v === null) {\n      _velocityClampManual = false;\n      VEL_CLAMP = Math.min(1.5, 1.0 / SCALE);\n      return VEL_CLAMP;\n    }\n    const n = +v;\n    if (!isFinite(n) || n <= 0) {\n      console.warn('velocityClamp: expected positive number or null, got', v);\n      return VEL_CLAMP;\n    }\n    VEL_CLAMP = n;\n    _velocityClampManual = true;\n    return VEL_CLAMP;\n  },",

    "text": "Shipped v0.84. New \"Max velocity\" slider in Settings, just below Resolution. Range 0.5\u20137.5, default auto-computed (~1.14 at your current resolution), step 0.05. The \u21bb button restores the auto-coupled default.\n\nWhat to try first:\n\n**Radial gravity + high Max velocity.** Pull = 50-100, Max velocity = 4 or higher. The radial pull becomes much more aggressive \u2014 splashes get torn apart and pushed outward at high speed. Could be the \"moody portfolio transition\" you wanted.\n\n**Deluge with Max velocity = 5 or 6.** The deluge already injects strong radial velocity; with a higher cap, the splash retains more of that energy before clamping, so you get a more violent initial burst that takes longer to dissipate.\n\n**Switch to \"standard\" advection mode + Max velocity above 1.7.** This is the \"see the artifacts\" mode. The cardinal cross should return, fairly dramatically. The docs prose now mentions this explicitly so you have a reference for what those artifacts look like.\n\nA note on UX: the slider step is 0.05 because at small values (around the default 1.14) finer adjustment matters more than at higher values. If 0.05 feels too coarse near 5-7, easy to widen the step to 0.1 above some threshold. Tell me how it feels.",

Verifying that mode='closed' conserves mass (modulo evaporation), mode='open' loses the expected fraction based on outflow flux, mode='gravity' loses more with higher Pull. I'll add a fifth pattern to the harness file.\n\n**Citations** \u2014 nothing strictly new is needed, but I should be explicit that the hybrid approach (semi-Lagrangian + donor-cell drainage) is described in Bridson Ch. 7 (\"Boundary Conditions\").       "description": "Insert Open Boundaries subsection in Dig Deeper",
      "new_str": "          singularity to begin with, and no pressure dip can form.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-open-boundaries\">Open boundaries: paint falling off the edge of the page</h2>\n        <p>\n          v0.81\u2013v0.84 added three edge-boundary modes\n          (closed, open, gravity) and a configurable velocity bias\n          with an outward-radial option. This required a small but\n          interesting hybrid of advection schemes, because the\n          natural way to think about outflow boundaries doesn't\n          fit the structure of semi-Lagrangian advection.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>The conceptual asymmetry</h3>\n        <p>\n          Semi-Lagrangian advection works <em>backward</em>: for each\n          destination cell, trace backward along the velocity vector\n          to find where the pigment came from one step ago, then\n          sample that source position with bilinear interpolation.\n          The scheme is naturally stable (no CFL bound) and naturally\n          isotropic (bilinear interp has no axis preference). Both\n          properties were what made the v0.67 switch to semi-Lag fix\n          the cross artifact.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          But outflow boundaries are inherently <em>forward</em>:\n          they describe where pigment <em>goes</em> when it leaves\n          the canvas. Semi-Lagrangian doesn't model that. A cell at\n          the boundary back-traces to an interior cell upstream and\n          keeps that pigment. There's no mechanism by which pigment\n          \"leaves\" the grid through this scheme, because the loop\n          iterates over destination cells, not source cells.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The first thing I tried was to modify the back-trace clamp:\n          when the back-trace landed outside an open edge, sample\n          \"background\" (zero pigment) instead of clamping to the\n          interior. This is the textbook open-boundary condition for\n          semi-Lagrangian and it works for some patterns &mdash; it\n          handles cells whose <em>back-trace</em> goes off-canvas,\n          which represents the case \"in the previous frame, this\n          cell's contents came from off-canvas, so we have no idea\n          what was there, so assume nothing.\" But the case I\n          actually needed was the opposite: cells whose\n          <em>forward</em> trajectory goes off-canvas. The back-trace\n          modification handles a real but secondary case.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>The hybrid: semi-Lagrangian interior, donor-cell drainage at edges</h3>\n        <p>\n          The fix was to add an explicit forward-flux drainage pass\n          on the cells adjacent to each open edge, after the main\n          semi-Lagrangian advection. For each such cell, compute the\n          outgoing normal flux:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-math\">flux_out = max(0, v_normal) \u00b7 \u0394t\npigment[i] *= (1 - flux_out)</pre>\n        <p>\n          where <em>v<sub>normal</sub></em> is the component of velocity\n          pointing across the boundary (positive y for the bottom\n          edge, positive x for the right, and so on). Capped at\n          <code>flux_out \u2264 1</code> so cells can't go negative. The\n          same fraction is applied to <code>g[]</code>, <code>d[]</code>,\n          <code>wet[]</code>, and <code>pressure[]</code> uniformly,\n          since whatever leaves the canvas takes everything with it.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          This is essentially a one-cell-deep donor-cell advection\n          step at the boundary, layered on top of the\n          semi-Lagrangian interior. It's a hybrid scheme, not\n          published anywhere I know of, but it follows naturally\n          from Bridson's treatment <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[5]</a> of boundary conditions in the\n          Advection Algorithms chapter. The interior keeps the\n          benefits of semi-Lagrangian (isotropy, stability); the\n          boundary gets the explicit forward-flux model that outflow\n          requires.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>The drainage propagates upstream gradually</h3>\n        <p>\n          Only cells directly adjacent to an open edge drain in any\n          given frame. Cells further inside drain over many frames\n          via the semi-Lagrangian back-trace pulling from\n          increasingly empty downstream cells. Concretely: a cell at\n          (x, GH-3) one row inside the drainage row back-traces to\n          some position upstream when it has upward velocity (no\n          drainage), but when it has downward velocity, the\n          back-trace position is upstream-and-down of itself, which\n          eventually corresponds to a cell at (x', GH-2). That cell\n          has just been drained, so the back-traced sample picks up\n          the reduced value and the cell at (x, GH-3) becomes\n          slightly emptier.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The net effect is drainage that propagates one cell per\n          frame upstream, mediated by the velocity field. With a\n          radial gravity bias, this happens from all four edges\n          simultaneously, producing the centrally-emptying effect\n          that gave radial gravity its visual appeal. Without bias,\n          drainage only occurs where the original splash velocity\n          pushed pigment toward an edge.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Why radial gravity drains 4\u00d7 faster than directional</h3>\n        <p>\n          Empirically, radial gravity at Pull=0.10 drains a centered\n          splash by 9.9% of total mass in 100 frames, vs 2.6% for\n          directional-down gravity at the same Pull. The ratio is\n          not coincidental.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          With directional gravity (say, \"down\"), only the bottom\n          edge is open. Pigment must traverse from wherever it is to\n          the bottom row before it can drain. The upper-half of the\n          canvas never directly contributes to drainage; it has to\n          first move down through the entire grid.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          With radial gravity, all four edges are open AND every\n          cell has bias pointing toward the nearest edge from its\n          position. A cell near the top-left has bias pointing up\n          and left; a cell at the bottom-right has bias pointing\n          down and right. Every cell contributes drainage flux to\n          its nearest edge. The total outflow surface area is 4\u00d7\n          larger, and the average distance from any cell to its\n          drainage edge is roughly half that of the directional case\n          (each cell's nearest edge vs the bottom edge specifically).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The 4\u00d7 ratio is the geometric consequence of having four\n          open edges instead of one, plus the per-cell radial bias\n          aiming every cell at its nearest edge.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Velocity clamp and CFL revisited</h3>\n        <p>\n          The v0.84 work exposed\n          <code>VEL_CLAMP</code> &mdash; the per-cell velocity\n          magnitude cap &mdash; as a user-facing slider. This is\n          worth a note because it reopens the CFL discussion from\n          the cross-artifact arc.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          First-order donor-cell advection is conditionally stable:\n          <code>|u|\u00b7\u0394t + |v|\u00b7\u0394t \u2264 1</code>. With\n          <code>\u0394t = DT \u00b7 0.7 \u2248 0.0117</code> and a 2D donor-cell\n          stencil, the practical bound is\n          <code>VEL_CLAMP \u2264 1.7</code> on a unit grid. The original\n          default <code>min(1.5, 2/SCALE)</code> was chosen to sit\n          comfortably below this bound at all supported resolutions.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Semi-Lagrangian has no CFL bound because the backward-trace\n          can land anywhere on the grid via bilinear sample. So\n          values of <code>VEL_CLAMP</code> from 2 up to ~7.5 are\n          numerically safe in semi-Lagrangian mode (above ~7.5 the\n          back-trace can skip over so many cells that bilinear\n          interp visibly smears, which is a stylistic limit rather\n          than a stability one). The new slider exposes this range,\n          and the docs warn that other advection modes will produce\n          the cross artifact above ~1.7.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The interaction with the gravity bias is also worth\n          flagging. The bias is added to <code>(nu, nv)</code> before\n          the magnitude clamp. So at high Pull values, the bias can\n          push velocity past <code>VEL_CLAMP</code> and the clamp\n          scales both components back down. Above Pull = 100 (where\n          the bias magnitude equals <code>VEL_CLAMP</code> itself),\n          increasing Pull doesn't make pigment move faster &mdash; it\n          just makes the bias arrive faster and dominate other forces\n          like splash velocity and pressure gradients. To actually\n          move pigment faster, raise <code>VEL_CLAMP</code> via the\n          new Max velocity slider.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-debug-lessons\">Debugging fluid simulations &mdash; lessons</h2>",

      "description": "Insert Pattern 5 after Pattern 4 and update section header to \"Five\"",
      "new_str": "        <h3>Pattern 4: CFL bound check (sanity check before changing scales)</h3>\n        <p>\n          For first-order donor-cell advection on a unit grid, the\n          stability condition is <code>(|u| + |v|)\u00b7\u0394t \u2264 1</code> per\n          cell. Computing the max over the active region tells you\n          whether the velocity scale + timestep combination will\n          stay stable. Useful when changing\n          <code>VEL_CLAMP</code>, <code>DT</code>, or splash velocity\n          defaults. Semi-Lagrangian has no CFL bound, so this check\n          is informational rather than required when running in\n          semilag mode.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Pattern 5: mass balance (verifies boundary-mode invariants, v0.84+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          Total mass tracking distinguishes the three edge modes\n          numerically. Sum <code>g[] + d[]</code> over the entire\n          grid before and after a sim run, and the change should\n          match the expected behavior for the active mode:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Closed mode:</strong> mass loss equals evaporation only. With evaporation disabled, mass should be conserved to within floating-point precision (~1e-6 relative drift). Any larger drift means the mass-conservation invariant of semi-Lagrangian with divergence correction is broken.</li>\n          <li><strong>Open mode:</strong> mass loss equals boundary drainage. The integral of outward normal flux over all four edges across all frames. With no pigment near any edge, mass loss is zero; with a splash near an edge, mass loss scales with the velocity-weighted boundary integral.</li>\n          <li><strong>Gravity mode, directional:</strong> mass loss scales with Pull (which sets the average outward velocity at the open edge) and with simulation time.</li>\n          <li><strong>Gravity mode, radial:</strong> mass loss is roughly 4\u00d7 the directional case at equal Pull (one open edge vs four, plus per-cell radial bias). Smoke-tested in v0.83 verification.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          This is the right pattern to use when adding new boundary\n          behaviors or changing the drainage flux formula. Closed\n          mode acts as a control (mass should still conserve); open\n          and gravity modes verify that the new drainage is doing\n          something measurable. The harness now includes this\n          pattern (run with <code>node washes-test-harness.js\n          /path/to/lib.js mass-balance</code>) and validates all\n          three modes plus a regression check that closed mode\n          hasn't been changed by the boundary work.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-bestpractices\">Best practices for fluid-sim debugging</h2>",

'UNSTABLE (donor-cell)' : 'stable';\n    console.log(`  ${sc.label.padEnd(40)} max=${maxCFL.toFixed(2)}  ${verdict}`);\n  }\n}\n\n// ===========================================================================\n// PATTERN 5 \u2014 Mass balance (verifies boundary-mode invariants, v0.84+)\n// ===========================================================================\n// Total mass tracking distinguishes the three edge modes numerically. The\n// pattern that validates the v0.81-v0.84 boundary work: closed conserves\n// mass (modulo evaporation), open drains based on outflow flux, gravity\n// drains more with higher Pull, radial drains ~4x faster than directional.\n// Used as a regression check that closed mode hasn't been changed by the\n// boundary work, and as a numerical sanity check that open/gravity modes\n// are doing something measurable.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction runMassBalance(libPath) {\n  installMockDOM();\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\n    _debug_simStep(n) { for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) simStep(); },\n    _debug_totalMass() {\n      let m = 0;\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n        m += g[0][i] + g[1][i] + g[2][i] + d[0][i] + d[1][i] + d[2][i];\n      }\n      return m;\n    },\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) d[0][i] = amt;\n      setActiveRectFull();\n    },`);\n\n  const scenarios = [\n    { mode: 'closed',  dir: 'down',   str: 0.00, expect: '~evaporation only (~2%)' },\n    { mode: 'open',    dir: 'down',   str: 0.00, expect: 'drainage from splash velocity (~3-5%)' },\n    { mode: 'gravity', dir: 'down',   str: 0.05, expect: 'mild downward drainage (~2-3%)' },\n    { mode: 'gravity', dir: 'down',   str: 0.10, expect: 'stronger downward drainage (~2-3%)' },\n    { mode: 'gravity', dir: 'radial', str: 0.05, expect: 'radial drainage from 4 edges (~5-8%)' },\n    { mode: 'gravity', dir: 'radial', str: 0.10, expect: 'aggressive radial drainage (~9-12%)' },\n  ];\n\n  console.log('\\n=== Mass balance over 100 simSteps (centered splash) ===');\n  console.log('  Closed should retain almost all mass; open/gravity should lose measurable fractions.');\n  for (const sc of scenarios) {\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\n    wc.edgeMode(sc.mode);\n    wc.gravityDirection(sc.dir);\n    wc.gravityStrength(sc.str);\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\n    wc.splash([{ x: 324, y: 270, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n    const m0 = wc._debug_totalMass();\n    wc._debug_simStep(100);\n    const m100 = wc._debug_totalMass();\n    const lossPct = ((m0 - m100) / m0 * 100).toFixed(1);\n    const label = `${sc.mode.padEnd(8)} dir=${sc.dir.padEnd(7)} str=${sc.str.toFixed(2)}`;\n    console.log(`  ${label}  \u2192  ${lossPct.padStart(5)}% loss   (expect: ${sc.expect})`);\n  }\n\n  // Sanity check: closed mode mass loss should be approximately equal to\n  // evaporation, not affected by gravity Pull (which only fires in\n  // gravity mode anyway).\n  console.log('\\n  Closed-mode regression: Pull should have no effect in closed mode');\n  for (const str of [0.0, 0.10, 0.50]) {\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\n    wc.edgeMode('closed');\n    wc.gravityStrength(str);  // ignored in closed mode\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\n    wc.splash([{ x: 324, y: 270, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n    const m0 = wc._debug_totalMass();\n    wc._debug_simStep(100);\n    const m100 = wc._debug_totalMass();\n    const lossPct = ((m0 - m100) / m0 * 100).toFixed(1);\n    console.log(`    closed, Pull=${str.toFixed(2)}: ${lossPct}% loss  (should match across all Pull values)`);\n  }\n}\n\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// CLI dispatcher\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nconst libPath = process.argv[2];\nconst pattern = process.argv[3] || 'all';\n\nif (!libPath) {\n  console.error('usage: node washes-test-harness.js <path-to-watercolor-lib.js> [pattern]');\n  console.error('patterns: anisotropy | trace | hotspots | cfl | mass-balance | all');\n  process.exit(1);\n}\nif (!fs.existsSync(libPath)) {\n  console.error('lib not found:', libPath);\n  process.exit(1);\n}\n\nconst patterns = {\n  anisotropy: runAnisotropy,\n  trace: runTrace,\n  hotspots: runHotspots,\n  cfl: runCFL,\n  'mass-balance': runMassBalance,\n};",

Right now drainage is one cell deep per frame; a more aggressive scheme would let drainage propagate multiple cells per frame, or use a different downwind scheme. That's interesting future work but not necessary for the current visual quality.     "text": " Before I generate files, let me think about what \"turn this into a library that supports TypeScript\" actually means in your case. There are several different things it could mean, with very different scopes.\n\n## What it could mean\n\n**Option A: Add type declarations (a `.d.ts` file) only.** The lib stays JavaScript \u2014 same source, same behavior \u2014 but TypeScript users get type checking and autocomplete when they import it. Smallest scope. Most TypeScript users would be perfectly happy with this.\n\n**Option B: Convert the lib to TypeScript source.** Rewrite `watercolor-lib.js` as `watercolor-lib.ts`, add a build step that compiles to `.js`. Bigger scope: changes how you author the code, requires a build pipeline, but you'd get type checking *during development* on the lib itself.\n\n**Option C: Package it for npm distribution.** Add `package.json`, `tsconfig.json`, build scripts, README, LICENSE, `.npmignore`, etc. Everything needed to `npm publish` a real library. Largest scope but the one that makes it actually consumable by other people.\n\nThese compose: A is the minimum for \"supports TypeScript,\" C usually includes A, B is optional and orthogonal to both.\n\n## My recommendation\n\n**A + C, not B.** Specifically:\n\n- Keep the lib as JavaScript (it's working; rewriting in TypeScript is busywork that risks bugs and gains little)\n- Add a hand-written `.d.ts` file that exports the public API surface \u2014 `Washes.create()`, the returned instance methods, the option types\n- Add the npm packaging files so you could publish if you wanted to (or just use the structure for organization)\n- Set up the directory layout that follows npm conventions\n\nA few reasons:\n- Hand-written `.d.ts` files are the standard pattern for JS libraries. React, lodash, every major framework historically did it this way. Type checking quality is excellent and the runtime stays untouched.\n- The lib is already working and validated. Rewriting in TS would require validating it all over again.\n- Most importantly: you'd need a build step (tsc) for B. That adds complexity to the dev loop. You don't currently have one \u2014 you just edit files and rebuild the HTML. Keep that.\n\n## What I'd actually generate\n\nA standard library layout:\n\n```\nwashes/\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 README.md                  # top-level project description, install, usage\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 LICENSE                    # MIT (we discussed earlier)\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 CITATION.cff               # academic citation file (auto-renders on GitHub)\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 package.json               # npm metadata\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 .npmignore                 # exclude HTML/test/dev from published package\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 .gitignore                 # standard\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 tsconfig.json              # for consumers who want to type-check imports\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 src/\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500\u2500 washes.js              # the lib (rename of watercolor-lib.js)\n\u2502   \u2514\u2500\u2500 washes.d.ts            # type declarations\n\u251c\u2500\u2500 tests/\n\u2502   \u2514\u2500\u2500 washes-test-harness.js # the existing harness\n\u2514\u2500\u2500 examples/\n    \u2514\u2500\u2500 playground.html        # the existing playground for demo purposes\n```\n\n## A few decisions before I do this\n\nDon't want to assume on these:\n\n1. **Library name.** \"Washes\" is what we've been calling it. For npm, lowercase is conventional: `washes`. But `washes` might be taken on npm \u2014 if so, `@stephanie/washes` (scoped to your username) or some variant. Want me to search npm first?\n\n2. **What's the public API I should type?** The lib has dozens of methods. I'd type:\n   - `Washes.create(element, options?)` and the full options interface\n   - The returned instance with all its methods (~40 of them based on what we've added: pigment, brushSize, splash, traceSVG, edgeMode, gravityDirection, gravityStrength, velocityClamp, etc.)\n   - Event types for `'rescaled'`\n   - Pigment indices, named brushes, advection modes, edge modes as string literal unions\n   \n   This is a meaningful amount of typing. Want me to be exhaustive (every method we've ever added), or just the most-used surface area (create, paint, splash, traceSVG, the toggles)?\n\n3. **Is this for actual npm publication, or just for your portfolio/GitHub repo?** That changes a few small choices \u2014 npm publication wants stricter versioning conventions, may need a `prepublish` script, etc. Portfolio/repo can skip some of that.\n\n4. **Module format.** ESM (`import { Washes } from 'washes'`) is the modern default. CommonJS (`require('washes')`) is what older Node code uses. UMD (works in both browsers and Node) is what your current lib is set up for (it attaches to `window.Washes`). I'd lean toward **exporting both ESM and the existing global** \u2014 embedders using a bundler get clean imports, and the playground HTML still works as-is.\n\n5. **License confirmation.** We discussed MIT earlier. Confirming that's what you want.\n\nOnce I have these answers I can generate the whole package in one pass. The actual work is straightforward \u2014 the planning is what determines whether the output is useful.",

Now that we\u2019re familiar with both the wet-on-dry and wet-on-wet methods, we\u2019re going to practice these washes on both dry and wet paper. A flat wash is a solid swatch of color. When painting a flat wash, you want to achieve a solid swatch of color.\n\nThen choose two colors at a time and try variegated washes. Even though it seems like you\u2019re not really painting anything worthwhile, it\u2019s only going to help you because you\u2019re building muscle memory, you\u2019re learning about your paint colors and supplies, and you\u2019re learning to let loose and have fun with no pressure to create anything specific. Thanks so much for reading! I hope you learned something new. Remember to have fun with these techniques. Half the fun with watercolor is literally going with the flow and observing what happens. Happy accidents are most welcome here, and I hope it helps you fall in love with watercolor more and more. There are more parts to this series, so stay tuned! I\u2019d love to hear from you. Please leave a comment down below and share about your experience with these techniques. If you try out these exercises, please tag me on social media @AudreyRaDesign and use the hashtag #PaintWithAudrey.\n\nIf you do see streaks in your wash, it could be that the paint dried too quickly before you could move it. Or if it gets lighter or too dark, then your mixture was inconsistent. So make sure that the water to paint ratio is consistent. That\u2019s why the flat wash on dry is better for a smaller area, unless you are using a large enough brush to quickly work. Next, let\u2019s do it on wet paper. When the paper is already wet, you have a better chance at covering larger areas because the paint will take longer to dry, and you have more time to spread the color out. Let\u2019s first wet our paper. Make sure that your paper is just wet enough. Not too dry and no water blobs. Then, pick up your paint, make sure that you\u2019re grabbing paint that is slightly watered down so that your brush is evenly coated and that you don\u2019t drop too much pigment in one area. Then slowly spread the color out with your brush in one directional strokes.\n\nA graded wash is a faded swatch of color. Similar to the flat wash on dry paper, you want to use a graded wash on a smaller area of dry paper. I use this technique a lot on my paintings. To do a graded wash on dry paper, start with a paint to water ratio of about 90 to 10. You want to start with as saturated of a color as possible. Then apply broad strokes back and forth. After a little bit, dip your brush in your water cup, and then blot quickly just to remove a little bit of excess water. Then come back to your wash and keep going. You\u2019ll notice that it\u2019s a lighter color. This time let\u2019s get an even lighter value. So wash out your brush a bit more, blot a bit more. And finish the swatch. Let\u2019s do it on wet paper. Start by wetting your paper as we did the flat wash. Then dry your brush. Pick up paint that is pretty saturated. Then drop it at the place you want your darkest area to be.",

        "text": "Learn how to create four basic watercolor washes - flat, gradient, variegated and wet-into-wet. Painting a successful watercolor doesn\u2019t have to be difficult or frustrating - the wash is the basis for each of your paintings.\n\nPainting a successful watercolor doesn\u2019t have to be difficult or frustrating. Once you have mastered the most integral part of the process, you will have a fresh and luminous painting every time. The wash is the basis for each of your paintings. It is color laid into an area usually too big to accomplish with only one stroke. Skies, backgrounds, and any space requiring an application of smooth or integrated color can be achieved by a successful wash. ... Flat wash \u2013 useful for skies and any area requiring smooth color with no visible brush strokes. Graduated or gradient wash \u2013 great for skies, soft transitions of light to dark or vice versa, used often in Asian watercolor and prints. Variegated wash \u2013 used when you need a transition of one color to another, like when painting a sunset where the color in the sky transitions from blue to orange.\n\nAlong with these four basic washes, there is also a glaze, which is a flat wash done with very diluted pigment. Thin, transparent layers are applied over an existing DRY wash. Knowing the properties of your pigments will help ensure a clean, translucent effect. Visit our Resources section to read your favorite Color Stories or review properties listed on our color charts. You can also make your own swatch chart to see how each pigment will appear on a piece of watercolor paper. ... Tape an eighth of a sheet of 300# watercolor paper (or 5X7\u201d sheet of 140#) to a backing board and place on a slant using a two or three-inch binder under it for support. Mix up a puddle of pigment much larger than you think you will need. Wet your paper (I use #6 Isabey 6235 series squirrel hair cat\u2019s tongue filbert wash brush). Do not wet it to the point of it being sopping wet (that will cause the wash to dry unevenly and may create runs and backwashes), but equally wet over the entire surface.\n\nTry different combinations, such as three different pigments or two complementary colors (opposite one another on the color wheel). Try three analogous colors and one complementary color (a color opposite one of your analogous colors on the color wheel). The possibilities are endless and limited by only your imagination. Once you have mastered these four basic washes, you can paint anything in watercolor! The most important thing is that you enjoy the process and remember that no matter where you are in your watercolor journey, take time to splash around! It\u2019s about process, not perfection. ... This website uses Google Translate to instantly and automatically translate content into multiple languages.\n\nLoad your brush with pigment and pull it evenly across the top of the paper. Begin on whichever side is most comfortable for you, but remember to start at the same edge each pass. When you reach the opposite edge and have completed my first stroke, repeat this step by reloading the brush and continuing with the next stroke just below the one above it. Gravity will help pull the color down, and if your paper is not too wet, your wash will begin to even out as you come to the bottom of your paper. Pick up any excess pigment and/or water at the edges of your wash with a slightly damp, pointed round brush or paper towel, being careful not to disturb your wash. When the shine has left your paper, you may set it flat to dry, being careful not to leave any excess moisture on your edges or on your tape. If you\u2019re following the steps and your paint doesn\u2019t lay smoothly on your paper \u2013 it\u2019s the paper. (Arches watercolor paper is great, but there are lots of less expensive brands that will work.) Stiff brushes push the paint down into the paper and leave brushstrokes. A soft wash or watercolor brush will gently lay the color on top of the paper.\n\nWash brushes come in flat, round and oval. (Hake brushes aren\u2019t expensive and the oval wash brush Silver Black Velvet is worth the money.) Probably not. Watercolor paper expands when it gets wet, so it will buckle if you tape the sides. Alternatives to taping \u2013 If you wet the back of your paper, it will stick to your backboard without tape, while it is wet. Or, use a circle on tape on the back of your paper to hold it to your backboard. Wet Paint on Dry Paper \u2013 Work quickly! Only go over the paper one time. Get in, get out, don\u2019t play with it. If you work quickly on dry paper, your paper should not buckle. Wet Paint on Wet Paper \u2013 wet both sides of your paper. (Wet as many times as you need or soak the paper in a tub of water for 5 or more minutes.) When the paper is evenly wet, it will lay flat and stick to your backboard. NO TAPE! If your wet paper still bubbles up, that means the back is not wet enough.\n\nSmooth flat watercolor washes must be done quickly, so there is no time to stop and try to mix more color \u2013 stopping in the middle is a recipe for disaster!) Mix a big puddle of color, and test the color on scrap paper. Add water to lighten or more paint to darken. Remember \u2013 most paints dry lighter than they look wet. (Qor brand watercolor paint by Golden Company doesn\u2019t fade as it dries.) Last, be sure there no lumps or chunks of color in your mix. Put a roll of paper towels or other handy object under the top of your backboard so your paper is tilted about 30 degrees. Big brush \u2013 Use as big a brush as your can for the area you\u2019re working on. Gently lay the paint on the surface in one stroke per area, or mop it on, but do not keep going over the same area. (The more brushstrokes you do, the more likely you are to have brushstrokes showing.)\n\nWhen you reach the bottom, wick up the bead of water with a paper towel or thirsty brush. If there is standing water around the edges, wipe it up with a paper towel. Otherwise, the standing water will be drawn back into your wash as the paper dries, creating a bloom. If you notice a loose hair in your wet watercolor wash \u2013 LEAVE IT ALONE! Do not try to remove it or you\u2019ll mess up your wash. After the wash dries, you can brush the hair off. If it left a line, retouch just the dark line with the very tip of damp watercolor brush and dab with a paper towel. Be patient and work carefully. You can lift up the line if you\u2019re very careful. Run a bead of hot glue along the brush ferrule. As soon as the glue is cool enough to touch, mash the glue into the bristles. This is a permanent fix to stop a brush from loosing hairs. This took MANY washes of color!",

Disclaimer: Jennifer Branch Gallery is a participant in the Amazon Services LLC Associates Program, an affiliate advertising program designed to provid\n\nWet paint onto dry paper is the wash I tend to use the most. A wet brush on dry paper allows the paint to flow randomly but in a defined area. You have complete control of everything the paint does. There's still room for happy accidents like backwashes when you let them happen, but the \"accidents\" happen where you want them to! The great advantage of wet paint onto dry paper watercolor wash is that you can leave holes in your washes. So you have whites that sparkle in the middle of darks. You have under layers that peek through in dashes. This wash needs more brush control, but it makes keeping those wonderful whites so much easier in the end. As with any watercolor wash, don't be wimpy when you're using water or paint. Remember paint always dries much lighter than when you put it on, so add more pigment than you think you need. If the pigment doesn't look translucent, at least a little, you need to add more water.\n\nYou can use gobs of pigment in watercolor, but not on a wet on dry paper wash.       "description": "TypeScript declarations for Washes \u2014 exhaustive public API",
      "path": "/home/claude/washes-pkg/src/washes.d.ts",
      "file_text": "// washes.d.ts\n//\n// TypeScript declarations for the Washes watercolor simulation library.\n//\n// The runtime is plain JavaScript (no compilation step). These declarations\n// describe the public API surface as it exists at the JS level. Internal\n// helpers, debug methods (`_debug_*`), and private state are intentionally\n// omitted.\n//\n// Authored against lib v0.85. When the lib adds new public methods, add\n// them here. Methods removed from the lib should be removed here too \u2014\n// keep this file in sync with the actual API.\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Core types\n// =============================================================================\n\n/** Pigment slot index. 0 = quinacridone rose, 1 = hansa yellow, 2 = cerulean blue. */\nexport type PigmentIndex = 0 | 1 | 2;\n\n/**\n * Named brushes that aren't tied to a pigment slot.\n *\n * - `'water'`: clear water; reactivates dry pigment and helps it spread.\n * - `'lift'`: removes pigment from cells it touches.\n * - `'rainbow'`: time-varying rainbow color from a phase-shifted palette.\n * - `'mask'`: paints into the freeze-mask layer (cells that flow skips).\n */\nexport type NamedBrush = 'water' | 'lift' | 'rainbow' | 'mask';\n\n/**\n * Anything pigment-shaped that the lib will accept. Numbers select a pigment\n * slot; strings select a slot by name OR select a special brush. The slot\n * names match the pigment palette in the docs.\n */\nexport type PigmentOption =\n  | PigmentIndex\n  | 'rose' | 'yellow' | 'blue'\n  | 'quinacridone-rose' | 'hansa-yellow' | 'cerulean-blue'\n  | NamedBrush;\n\n/** Advection scheme used by `movePigment()`. */\nexport type AdvectionMode = 'standard' | 'clamp' | 'substep' | 'semilag';\n\n/**\n * Edge boundary behavior. See the Open Boundaries section in the docs.\n *\n * - `'closed'`: walls reflect (mass conserved).\n * - `'open'`: all edges drain; no ambient bias.\n * - `'gravity'`: edges open in the direction of gravity, with active bias.\n */\nexport type EdgeMode = 'closed' | 'open' | 'gravity';\n\n/**\n * Direction of the gravity velocity bias. 8-compass plus `'radial'`,\n * which per-cell points outward from canvas center.\n */\nexport type GravityDirection =\n  | 'up' | 'up-right' | 'right' | 'down-right'\n  | 'down' | 'down-left' | 'left' | 'up-left'\n  | 'radial';\n\n/**\n * Gouache rendering mode.\n *\n * - `false`: pure watercolor (translucent pigments).\n * - `true`: opaque gouache pigments.\n * - `'auto'`: LERP between watercolor and gouache based on paper darkness.\n */\nexport type GouacheMode = boolean | 'auto';\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Splash / brush input shapes\n// =============================================================================\n\n/**\n * One epicenter for a splash. Coordinates are in display pixels relative to\n * the canvas host element. Velocity controls outward radial speed.\n */\nexport interface SplashEpicenter {\n  x: number;\n  y: number;\n  /** Outward radial velocity at the epicenter. Default ~40. */\n  velocity?: number;\n}\n\n/**\n * Options for a single splash call. Anything not specified falls back to the\n * library's current configuration (radius, pressure, etc.).\n */\nexport interface SplashOptions {\n  /** Outer ring radius in display pixels. */\n  radius?: number;\n  /** Peak pressure at the epicenter. Drives the initial outward push. */\n  pressure?: number;\n  /** Per-frame pressure decay multiplier. Range (0, 1]. Default ~0.94. */\n  liftRate?: number;\n  /** Angular offset for ray patterns, radians. */\n  angleOffset?: number;\n  /** Random jitter on epicenter position, 0..1. */\n  jitter?: number;\n  /** Number of rays in the splash pattern. 1 = pure circular. */\n  rays?: number;\n  /** Pigment used by this splash. Default = active brush pigment. */\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\n}\n\n/** Style of splash. Different presets shape the radial profile differently. */\nexport type SplashStyle = 'deluge' | 'splash' | 'spray';\n\n// =============================================================================\n// SVG / image / text rendering\n// =============================================================================\n\n/**\n * Options for `traceSVG`. The lib parses the SVG, decomposes it into\n * pen-stroke paths, and animates drawing them onto the canvas.\n */\nexport interface TraceSVGOptions {\n  /** Pigment to use for paths without a recognized fill/stroke color. */\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\n  /** Display-pixel brush size while tracing. */\n  brushSize?: number;\n  /** Stroke speed in display pixels per second. */\n  speed?: number;\n  /** Recognize chroma-key and pigment-hex colors as direct pigment assignments. Default true. */\n  triggerColors?: boolean;\n  /** Mirror the trace horizontally. */\n  flipX?: boolean;\n  /** Mirror the trace vertically. */\n  flipY?: boolean;\n  /** Multiplicative scale for the SVG path coordinates. */\n  scale?: number;\n  /** Translation in display pixels. */\n  translateX?: number;\n  translateY?: number;\n  /** Called once when the trace completes. */\n  onComplete?: () => void;\n}\n\n/** Options for `paintImage`. Source can be a URL, a data URL, or an HTMLImageElement. */\nexport interface PaintImageOptions {\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\n  brushSize?: number;\n  /** 0..1 threshold for which pixels become pigment. */\n  threshold?: number;\n  flipX?: boolean;\n  flipY?: boolean;\n  /** Reverse light/dark mapping. */\n  invert?: boolean;\n  scale?: number;\n  translateX?: number;\n  translateY?: number;\n  onComplete?: () => void;\n}\n\n/** Options for `paintText`. */\nexport interface PaintTextOptions {\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\n  brushSize?: number;\n  fontSize?: number;\n  fontFamily?: string;\n  x?: number;\n  y?: number;\n  onComplete?: () => void;\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Animation, visualization, time-wash\n// =============================================================================\n\nexport type AnimationName = 'off' | 'breathe' | 'drift' | 'pulse' | string;\nexport interface AnimationOptions {\n  speed?: number;\n  amplitude?: number;\n}\n\nexport type VisualizationName = 'off' | 'velocity' | 'pressure' | 'wet' | 'mask' | string;\nexport interface VisualizationOptions {\n  opacity?: number;\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Sketch / obliterate / fade\n// =============================================================================\n\nexport interface SketchModeOptions {\n  enabled?: boolean;\n  strength?: number;\n  paperContrast?: number;\n}\n\nexport interface ObliterateOptions {\n  /** Duration of the obliterate animation, ms. */\n  duration?: number;\n  /** Easing function: 'linear' | 'ease-out' | 'cubic' or a custom (t: number) => number. */\n  easing?: string | ((t: number) => number);\n  onComplete?: () => void;\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Performance metrics\n// =============================================================================\n\nexport interface PerfMetrics {\n  fps: number;\n  p50: number;\n  p95: number;\n  p99: number;\n  activeCells: number;\n  activeCellsPct: number;\n  heapDeltaMB: number;\n  longAnimationFrames: number;\n  perPhaseMs: {\n    diffuse: number;\n    edge: number;\n    velocity: number;\n    advection: number;\n    transfer: number;\n    render: number;\n  };\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Serializable preset (state subset; not a snapshot of pigment fields)\n// =============================================================================\n\n/**\n * Serializable subset of the lib's configuration. Round-trippable via\n * `getPreset()` and `applyPreset()`. Used for \"save my current settings\"\n * UX. Does NOT capture pigment fields \u2014 for that, use the canvas as an\n * image or call `state()` (which returns Float32Arrays not suitable for JSON).\n */\nexport interface Preset {\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\n  brushSize?: number;\n  paperWetness?: string;\n  evaporation?: number;\n  edgeMode?: EdgeMode;\n  gravityDirection?: GravityDirection;\n  gravityStrength?: number;\n  velocityClamp?: number;\n  gouacheMode?: GouacheMode;\n  advectionMode?: AdvectionMode;\n  maskTint?: boolean;\n  webgl?: boolean;\n  // Forward-compat: unknown keys are preserved through round-trip.\n  [key: string]: unknown;\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Constructor options\n// =============================================================================\n\n/**\n * Options passed to `Washes.create()`. All are optional \u2014 sensible defaults\n * are computed from the target element's size and the user's viewport.\n */\nexport interface CreateOptions {\n  /** Pixels per simulation cell. Higher = lower-res, faster. Default = 1.75. URL `?scale=` also accepted. */\n  scale?: number;\n  /** Canvas DPI scale factor. Default = window.devicePixelRatio. */\n  canvasScale?: number;\n  /** Force mobile-mode heuristics (palm rejection, etc.). Default auto-detected via `matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')`. */\n  mobile?: boolean;\n  /** Show floating cursor preview circle. Default true on desktop. */\n  cursorPreview?: boolean;\n  /** Initial gouache mode. Default `false`. Pass `'auto'` for paper-darkness-driven LERP. */\n  gouacheMode?: GouacheMode;\n  /** Continuous flow during paint strokes. Default true. */\n  continuousFlow?: boolean;\n  /** Disable pointer event handling entirely. Useful for programmatic control. Default true. */\n  pointer?: boolean;\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Returned instance\n// =============================================================================\n\n/**\n * Most setters return `this` to support chained calls. Getters return the\n * current value. The `(v?: T)` pattern throughout \u2014 pass nothing to read,\n * pass a value to set.\n */\nexport interface WashesInstance {\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Painting input\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Inject a splash at one or more epicenters. */\n  splash(epicenters: SplashEpicenter[], style?: SplashStyle, opts?: SplashOptions): WashesInstance;\n\n  /**\n   * Programmatic paint stamp at grid coordinates (not display coordinates).\n   * Use `toGrid(displayX, displayY)` to convert if you have display coords.\n   */\n  paintAt(gx: number, gy: number, gridRadius: number, pigment?: PigmentOption, strength?: number): WashesInstance;\n\n  /** Stamp text using the active brush. */\n  paintText(text: string, opts?: PaintTextOptions): Promise<void>;\n\n  /** Stamp an image using the active brush (light/dark map to pigment density). */\n  paintImage(source: string | HTMLImageElement, opts?: PaintImageOptions): Promise<void>;\n\n  /** Trace an SVG, drawing each path with the active brush over time. */\n  traceSVG(svgText: string, opts?: TraceSVGOptions): Promise<void>;\n  /** Cancel any in-flight SVG trace. */\n  cancelSVGTrace(): WashesInstance;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Brush state\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Current brush diameter in display pixels. Default 28. */\n  brushSize(v?: number): number;\n\n  /** Active pigment (or named brush) for subsequent paint operations. */\n  pigment(v?: PigmentOption): PigmentOption | WashesInstance;\n\n  /** Read-only list of available pigment indices. */\n  pigments(): PigmentIndex[];\n\n  /** Paint load: how much pigment is delivered per stamp. 0..1. */\n  paintLoad(v?: number): number;\n\n  /** Water load: how much water is delivered per stamp. 0..1. */\n  waterLoad(v?: number): number;\n\n  /** Brush pressure: epicenter intensity multiplier. */\n  pressure(v?: number): number;\n\n  /** Flow rate: how quickly pigment spreads after stamping. */\n  flow(v?: number): number;\n\n  /** Use pointer pressure (stylus) to modulate flow. Default true if supported. */\n  usePointerPressure(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Whether dragging produces continuous flow or per-stamp dots. Default true. */\n  continuousFlow(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Paper state\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Set paper wetness preset (changes evaporation rate among other things). */\n  paperWetness(preset?: string): string;\n  /** List available wetness presets. */\n  paperWetnessPresets(): string[];\n\n  /** Direct evaporation rate. Higher = wash dries faster. */\n  evaporation(value?: number): number;\n\n  /** Pause/resume the drying simulation. */\n  pauseDrying(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Set paper color in 0..1 RGB. */\n  paperColor(r: number, g: number, b: number): WashesInstance;\n\n  /** Read the current rainbow brush color (for hover-state UI). */\n  rainbowColor(): { r: number; g: number; b: number };\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Edge boundaries (v0.81-v0.84)\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Edge boundary mode: closed, open, or gravity. */\n  edgeMode(v?: EdgeMode): EdgeMode;\n\n  /** Gravity direction (8-compass plus 'radial'). Ignored in `closed` mode. */\n  gravityDirection(v?: GravityDirection): GravityDirection;\n\n  /** Gravity bias strength. 0 = no pull; ~0.10 = strong tilt; ~1.0 = max. */\n  gravityStrength(v?: number): number;\n\n  /**\n   * Per-cell velocity magnitude cap. Auto-computed from `scale` by default\n   * (so display speed is resolution-invariant). Pass `null` to restore the\n   * auto-computed default.\n   *\n   * Note: values above ~1.7 require semi-Lagrangian advection mode; donor-\n   * cell modes will produce the cardinal-cross artifact above that bound.\n   */\n  velocityClamp(v?: number | null): number;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Rendering / display\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Toggle gouache rendering mode. */\n  gouacheMode(v?: GouacheMode): GouacheMode;\n  /** Current LERP amount in 'auto' gouache mode. 0 = full watercolor, 1 = full gouache. */\n  gouacheLerpAmount(): number;\n\n  /** Advection scheme. */\n  advectionMode(v?: AdvectionMode): AdvectionMode;\n  /** Substeps used by the most recent movePigment call. */\n  advectionLastSubsteps(): number;\n\n  /** Whether edge darkening is applied. Default true. */\n  edgeDarkening(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Show the amber tint over masked cells. Default true. */\n  maskTint(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Toggle WebGL rendering. Falls back to CPU if WebGL2 isn't supported. */\n  webgl(v?: boolean): boolean;\n  /** Whether WebGL2 was available at init. */\n  webglAvailable(): boolean;\n  /** Debug: tint visible cells green so you can see the active region. */\n  webglDebugTint(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Background transparent (canvas paper rendered transparent). */\n  transparent(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Paper color/texture fade applied each frame to existing painted area. */\n  fadePainting(v?: number): number;\n  /** Half-life of the fade-out (ms). */\n  fadeHalfLife(ms?: number): number;\n  /** Auto-fade the background between strokes. */\n  autoDryBackground(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  /** Canvas resolution: display pixels per sim cell. Triggers a rescale event. */\n  scale(v?: number): number;\n  /** Canvas DPI scale factor. */\n  canvasScale(v?: number): number;\n\n  /** Convert display coordinates to sim grid coordinates. */\n  toGrid(displayX: number, displayY: number): { gx: number; gy: number };\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Mask\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Clear the freeze mask. */\n  removeMask(): WashesInstance;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Composition modes\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Sketch mode (paper-contrasting line drawing overlay). */\n  sketchMode(opts?: SketchModeOptions): SketchModeOptions;\n\n  /** Obliterate animation: progressively destroys the painting. */\n  obliterate(opts?: ObliterateOptions): Promise<void>;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Background animation / visualization\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Set a named time-driven background wash. */\n  setBackground(name: string): WashesInstance;\n  isBackgroundRunning(): boolean;\n\n  /** Toggle an animation mode (breathe, drift, pulse, etc.). */\n  setAnimation(name: AnimationName, opts?: AnimationOptions): WashesInstance;\n  getAnimation(): AnimationName;\n\n  /** Toggle a debug visualization (velocity vectors, pressure heat, etc.). */\n  setVisualization(name: VisualizationName, opts?: VisualizationOptions): WashesInstance;\n  getVisualization(): VisualizationName;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Mobile / input\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Force mobile-mode heuristics. */\n  mobile(v?: boolean): boolean;\n  /** Was mobile auto-detected at init? */\n  mobileDetected(): boolean;\n\n  /** Show floating cursor preview circle. */\n  cursorPreview(v?: boolean): boolean;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Performance\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Start/stop perf instrumentation. */\n  perf(v?: boolean): boolean;\n  /** Current perf snapshot. */\n  perfMetrics(): PerfMetrics;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // UI helpers\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Build pigment swatch DOM elements into the given root. */\n  buildPigmentSwatches(rootEl: HTMLElement): void;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Export\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Export the current canvas as a PNG blob. */\n  exportPNG(opts?: { width?: number; height?: number; transparent?: boolean }): Promise<Blob>;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Preset / state\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Serializable subset of the current configuration. */\n  getPreset(): Preset;\n  /** Apply a previously-saved preset. */\n  applyPreset(preset: Preset): WashesInstance;\n\n  /** Internal state object for debugging. Shape is intentionally unspecified. */\n  state(): Record<string, unknown>;\n\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Lifecycle\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n  /** Tear down event listeners and free resources. */\n  destroy(): void;\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Static API\n// =============================================================================\n\n/**\n * The Washes constructor namespace, as imported from `'washes'` or as the\n * `window.Washes` global. The lib doesn't expose a `class Washes`; instead\n * it exposes a `create()` factory function on this object.\n */\nexport interface WashesStatic {\n  /**\n   * Create a Washes instance attached to the given DOM element. The element\n   * is treated as a host: a canvas is appended into it and sized to fill.\n   *\n   * @example\n   * import { Washes } from 'washes';\n   * const wc = Washes.create(document.getElementById('app')!, {\n   *   gouacheMode: 'auto',\n   * });\n   * wc.splash([{ x: 200, y: 200, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n   */\n  create(target: HTMLElement, options?: CreateOptions): WashesInstance;\n\n  /** Library version, e.g. \"0.85.0\". */\n  readonly version: string;\n}\n\n/** Default export: the WashesStatic namespace. */\ndeclare const Washes: WashesStatic;\nexport { Washes };\nexport default Washes;\n\n// =============================================================================\n// Global augmentation \u2014 the lib also attaches `window.Washes` for use in\n// no-bundler / classic-script contexts. TypeScript users who pull the lib\n// via a script tag rather than `import` can rely on this global.\n// =============================================================================\n\ndeclare global {\n  interface Window {\n    Washes: WashesStatic;\n  }\n}\n"

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"typescript\", \"code\": \"// washes.d.ts\\n//\\n// TypeScript declarations for the Washes watercolor simulation library.\\n//\\n// The runtime is plain JavaScript (no compilation step). These declarations\\n// describe the public API surface as it exists at the JS level. Internal\\n// helpers, debug methods (`_debug_*`), and private state are intentionally\\n// omitted.\\n//\\n// Authored against lib v0.85. When the lib adds new public methods, add\\n// them here. Methods removed from the lib should be removed here too \\u2014\\n// keep this file in sync with the actual API.\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Core types\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\n/** Pigment slot index. 0 = quinacridone rose, 1 = hansa yellow, 2 = cerulean blue. */\\nexport type PigmentIndex = 0 | 1 | 2;\\n\\n/**\\n * Named brushes that aren't tied to a pigment slot.\\n *\\n * - `'water'`: clear water; reactivates dry pigment and helps it spread.\\n * - `'lift'`: removes pigment from cells it touches.\\n * - `'rainbow'`: time-varying rainbow color from a phase-shifted palette.\\n * - `'mask'`: paints into the freeze-mask layer (cells that flow skips).\\n */\\nexport type NamedBrush = 'water' | 'lift' | 'rainbow' | 'mask';\\n\\n/**\\n * Anything pigment-shaped that the lib will accept. Numbers select a pigment\\n * slot; strings select a slot by name OR select a special brush. The slot\\n * names match the pigment palette in the docs.\\n */\\nexport type PigmentOption =\\n  | PigmentIndex\\n  | 'rose' | 'yellow' | 'blue'\\n  | 'quinacridone-rose' | 'hansa-yellow' | 'cerulean-blue'\\n  | NamedBrush;\\n\\n/** Advection scheme used by `movePigment()`. */\\nexport type AdvectionMode = 'standard' | 'clamp' | 'substep' | 'semilag';\\n\\n/**\\n * Edge boundary behavior. See the Open Boundaries section in the docs.\\n *\\n * - `'closed'`: walls reflect (mass conserved).\\n * - `'open'`: all edges drain; no ambient bias.\\n * - `'gravity'`: edges open in the direction of gravity, with active bias.\\n */\\nexport type EdgeMode = 'closed' | 'open' | 'gravity';\\n\\n/**\\n * Direction of the gravity velocity bias. 8-compass plus `'radial'`,\\n * which per-cell points outward from canvas center.\\n */\\nexport type GravityDirection =\\n  | 'up' | 'up-right' | 'right' | 'down-right'\\n  | 'down' | 'down-left' | 'left' | 'up-left'\\n  | 'radial';\\n\\n/**\\n * Gouache rendering mode.\\n *\\n * - `false`: pure watercolor (translucent pigments).\\n * - `true`: opaque gouache pigments.\\n * - `'auto'`: LERP between watercolor and gouache based on paper darkness.\\n */\\nexport type GouacheMode = boolean | 'auto';\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Splash / brush input shapes\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\n/**\\n * One epicenter for a splash. Coordinates are in display pixels relative to\\n * the canvas host element. Velocity controls outward radial speed.\\n */\\nexport interface SplashEpicenter {\\n  x: number;\\n  y: number;\\n  /** Outward radial velocity at the epicenter. Default ~40. */\\n  velocity?: number;\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * Options for a single splash call. Anything not specified falls back to the\\n * library's current configuration (radius, pressure, etc.).\\n */\\nexport interface SplashOptions {\\n  /** Outer ring radius in display pixels. */\\n  radius?: number;\\n  /** Peak pressure at the epicenter. Drives the initial outward push. */\\n  pressure?: number;\\n  /** Per-frame pressure decay multiplier. Range (0, 1]. Default ~0.94. */\\n  liftRate?: number;\\n  /** Angular offset for ray patterns, radians. */\\n  angleOffset?: number;\\n  /** Random jitter on epicenter position, 0..1. */\\n  jitter?: number;\\n  /** Number of rays in the splash pattern. 1 = pure circular. */\\n  rays?: number;\\n  /** Pigment used by this splash. Default = active brush pigment. */\\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\\n}\\n\\n/** Style of splash. Different presets shape the radial profile differently. */\\nexport type SplashStyle = 'deluge' | 'splash' | 'spray';\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// SVG / image / text rendering\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\n/**\\n * Options for `traceSVG`. The lib parses the SVG, decomposes it into\\n * pen-stroke paths, and animates drawing them onto the canvas.\\n */\\nexport interface TraceSVGOptions {\\n  /** Pigment to use for paths without a recognized fill/stroke color. */\\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\\n  /** Display-pixel brush size while tracing. */\\n  brushSize?: number;\\n  /** Stroke speed in display pixels per second. */\\n  speed?: number;\\n  /** Recognize chroma-key and pigment-hex colors as direct pigment assignments. Default true. */\\n  triggerColors?: boolean;\\n  /** Mirror the trace horizontally. */\\n  flipX?: boolean;\\n  /** Mirror the trace vertically. */\\n  flipY?: boolean;\\n  /** Multiplicative scale for the SVG path coordinates. */\\n  scale?: number;\\n  /** Translation in display pixels. */\\n  translateX?: number;\\n  translateY?: number;\\n  /** Called once when the trace completes. */\\n  onComplete?: () => void;\\n}\\n\\n/** Options for `paintImage`. Source can be a URL, a data URL, or an HTMLImageElement. */\\nexport interface PaintImageOptions {\\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\\n  brushSize?: number;\\n  /** 0..1 threshold for which pixels become pigment. */\\n  threshold?: number;\\n  flipX?: boolean;\\n  flipY?: boolean;\\n  /** Reverse light/dark mapping. */\\n  invert?: boolean;\\n  scale?: number;\\n  translateX?: number;\\n  translateY?: number;\\n  onComplete?: () => void;\\n}\\n\\n/** Options for `paintText`. */\\nexport interface PaintTextOptions {\\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\\n  brushSize?: number;\\n  fontSize?: number;\\n  fontFamily?: string;\\n  x?: number;\\n  y?: number;\\n  onComplete?: () => void;\\n}\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Animation, visualization, time-wash\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\nexport type AnimationName = 'off' | 'breathe' | 'drift' | 'pulse' | string;\\nexport interface AnimationOptions {\\n  speed?: number;\\n  amplitude?: number;\\n}\\n\\nexport type VisualizationName = 'off' | 'velocity' | 'pressure' | 'wet' | 'mask' | string;\\nexport interface VisualizationOptions {\\n  opacity?: number;\\n}\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Sketch / obliterate / fade\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\nexport interface SketchModeOptions {\\n  enabled?: boolean;\\n  strength?: number;\\n  paperContrast?: number;\\n}\\n\\nexport interface ObliterateOptions {\\n  /** Duration of the obliterate animation, ms. */\\n  duration?: number;\\n  /** Easing function: 'linear' | 'ease-out' | 'cubic' or a custom (t: number) => number. */\\n  easing?: string | ((t: number) => number);\\n  onComplete?: () => void;\\n}\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Performance metrics\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\nexport interface PerfMetrics {\\n  fps: number;\\n  p50: number;\\n  p95: number;\\n  p99: number;\\n  activeCells: number;\\n  activeCellsPct: number;\\n  heapDeltaMB: number;\\n  longAnimationFrames: number;\\n  perPhaseMs: {\\n    diffuse: number;\\n    edge: number;\\n    velocity: number;\\n    advection: number;\\n    transfer: number;\\n    render: number;\\n  };\\n}\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Serializable preset All are optional \\u2014 sensible defaults\\n * are computed from the target element's size and the user's viewport.\\n */\\nexport interface CreateOptions {\\n  /** Pixels per simulation cell.
