Default true on desktop. */\\n  cursorPreview?: boolean;\\n  /** Initial gouache mode. Default `false`. Pass `'auto'` for paper-darkness-driven LERP. */\\n  gouacheMode?: GouacheMode;\\n  /** Continuous flow during paint strokes. Default true. */\\n  continuousFlow?: boolean;\\n  /** Disable pointer event handling entirely. Useful for programmatic control. Default true. */\\n  pointer?: boolean;\\n}\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Returned instance\\n// =============================================================================\\n\\n/**\\n * Most setters return `this` to support chained calls. Getters return the\\n * current value. The `(v?: T)` pattern throughout \\u2014 pass nothing to read,\\n * pass a value to set.\\n */\\nexport interface WashesInstance {\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Painting input\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Inject a splash at one or more epicenters. */\\n  splash(epicenters: SplashEpicenter[], style?: SplashStyle, opts?: SplashOptions): WashesInstance;\\n\\n  /**\\n   * Programmatic paint stamp at grid coordinates (not display coordinates).\\n   * Use `toGrid(displayX, displayY)` to convert if you have display coords.\\n   */\\n  paintAt(gx: number, gy: number, gridRadius: number, pigment?: PigmentOption, strength?: number): WashesInstance;\\n\\n  /** Stamp text using the active brush. */\\n  paintText(text: string, opts?: PaintTextOptions): Promise<void>;\\n\\n  /** Stamp an image using the active brush (light/dark map to pigment density). */\\n  paintImage(source: string | HTMLImageElement, opts?: PaintImageOptions): Promise<void>;\\n\\n  /** Trace an SVG, drawing each path with the active brush over time. */\\n  traceSVG(svgText: string, opts?: TraceSVGOptions): Promise<void>;\\n  /** Cancel any in-flight SVG trace. */\\n  cancelSVGTrace(): WashesInstance;\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Brush state\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Current brush diameter in display pixels. Default 28. */\\n  brushSize(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Active pigment (or named brush) for subsequent paint operations. */\\n  pigment(v?: PigmentOption): PigmentOption | WashesInstance;\\n\\n  /** Read-only list of available pigment indices. */\\n  pigments(): PigmentIndex[];\\n\\n  /** Paint load: how much pigment is delivered per stamp. 0..1. */\\n  paintLoad(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Water load: how much water is delivered per stamp. 0..1. */\\n  waterLoad(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Brush pressure: epicenter intensity multiplier. */\\n  pressure(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Flow rate: how quickly pigment spreads after stamping. */\\n  flow(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Use pointer pressure (stylus) to modulate flow. Default true if supported. */\\n  usePointerPressure(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Whether dragging produces continuous flow or per-stamp dots. Default true. */\\n  continuousFlow(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Paper state\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Set paper wetness preset (changes evaporation rate among other things). */\\n  paperWetness(preset?: string): string;\\n  /** List available wetness presets. */\\n  paperWetnessPresets(): string[];\\n\\n  /** Direct evaporation rate. Higher = wash dries faster. */\\n  evaporation(value?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Pause/resume the drying simulation. */\\n  pauseDrying(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Set paper color in 0..1 RGB. */\\n  paperColor(r: number, g: number, b: number): WashesInstance;\\n\\n  /** Read the current rainbow brush color (for hover-state UI). */\\n  rainbowColor(): { r: number; g: number; b: number };\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Edge boundaries (v0.81-v0.84)\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Edge boundary mode: closed, open, or gravity. */\\n  edgeMode(v?: EdgeMode): EdgeMode;\\n\\n  /** Gravity direction (8-compass plus 'radial'). Ignored in `closed` mode. */\\n  gravityDirection(v?: GravityDirection): GravityDirection;\\n\\n  /** Gravity bias strength. 0 = no pull; ~0.10 = strong tilt; ~1.0 = max. */\\n  gravityStrength(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /**\\n   * Per-cell velocity magnitude cap. Auto-computed from `scale` by default\\n   * (so display speed is resolution-invariant). Pass `null` to restore the\\n   * auto-computed default.\\n   *\\n   * Note: values above ~1.7 require semi-Lagrangian advection mode; donor-\\n   * cell modes will produce the cardinal-cross artifact above that bound.\\n   */\\n  velocityClamp(v?: number | null): number;\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Rendering / display\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Toggle gouache rendering mode. */\\n  gouacheMode(v?: GouacheMode): GouacheMode;\\n  /** Current LERP amount in 'auto' gouache mode. 0 = full watercolor, 1 = full gouache. */\\n  gouacheLerpAmount(): number;\\n\\n  /** Advection scheme. */\\n  advectionMode(v?: AdvectionMode): AdvectionMode;\\n  /** Substeps used by the most recent movePigment call. */\\n  advectionLastSubsteps(): number;\\n\\n  /** Whether edge darkening is applied. Default true. */\\n  edgeDarkening(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Show the amber tint over masked cells. Default true. */\\n  maskTint(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Toggle WebGL rendering. Falls back to CPU if WebGL2 isn't supported. */\\n  webgl(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n  /** Whether WebGL2 was available at init. */\\n  webglAvailable(): boolean;\\n  /** Debug: tint visible cells green so you can see the active region. */\\n  webglDebugTint(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Background transparent (canvas paper rendered transparent). */\\n  transparent(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Paper color/texture fade applied each frame to existing painted area. */\\n  fadePainting(v?: number): number;\\n  /** Half-life of the fade-out (ms). */\\n  fadeHalfLife(ms?: number): number;\\n  /** Auto-fade the background between strokes. */\\n  autoDryBackground(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Canvas resolution: display pixels per sim cell. Triggers a rescale event. */\\n  scale(v?: number): number;\\n  /** Canvas DPI scale factor. */\\n  canvasScale(v?: number): number;\\n\\n  /** Convert display coordinates to sim grid coordinates. */\\n  toGrid(displayX: number, displayY: number): { gx: number; gy: number };\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Mask\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Clear the freeze mask. */\\n  removeMask(): WashesInstance;\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Composition modes\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Sketch mode (paper-contrasting line drawing overlay). */\\n  sketchMode(opts?: SketchModeOptions): SketchModeOptions;\\n\\n  /** Obliterate animation: progressively destroys the painting. */\\n  obliterate(opts?: ObliterateOptions): Promise<void>;\\n\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // Background animation / visualization\\n  // -----------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\n  /** Set a named time-driven background wash. */\\n  setBackground(name: string): WashesInstance;\\n mobileDetected(): boolean;\\n\\n  /** Show floating cursor preview circle. */\\n  cursorPreview(v?: boolean): boolean;\\n\\n  // The lib doesn't expose a `class Washes`; instead\\n * it exposes a `create()` factory function on this object.\\n */\\nexport interface WashesStatic {\\n  /**\\n   * Create a Washes instance attached to the given DOM element. The element\\n   * is treated as a host: a canvas is appended into it and sized to fill.\\n   *\\n   * @example\\n   * import { Washes } from 'washes';\\n   * const wc = Washes.create(document.getElementById('app')!, {\\n   *   gouacheMode: 'auto',\\n   * });\\n   * wc.splash([{ x: 200, y: 200, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\\n   */\\n  create(target: HTMLElement, options?: CreateOptions): WashesInstance;\\n\\n  /** Library version, e.g. \\\"0.85.0\\\". */\\n  readonly version: string;\\n}\\n\\n/** Default export: the WashesStatic namespace. */\\ndeclare const Washes: WashesStatic;\\nexport { Washes };\\nexport default Washes;\\n\\n// =============================================================================\\n// Global augmentation \\u2014 the lib also attaches `window.Washes` for use in\\n// no-bundler / classic-script contexts.       "description": "README that doubles as npm landing page",
      "file_text": "# Washes\n\nAn interactive watercolor simulation library for the browser. Built on Curtis et al.'s 1997 SIGGRAPH paper \"Computer-Generated Watercolor,\" with a three-layer fluid + pigment + paper model and Kubelka\u2013Munk optical compositing.\n\n```bash\nnpm install washes\n```\n\n```js\nimport { Washes } from 'washes';\n\nconst wc = Washes.create(document.getElementById('canvas-host'), {\n  gouacheMode: 'auto',\n});\n\nwc.splash([{ x: 200, y: 200, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\n## What it does\n\n- Physics-based watercolor simulation running at 60fps in the browser\n- Three pigments (quinacridone rose, hansa yellow, cerulean blue) composited via Kubelka\u2013Munk\n- Brushes, splashes, lifts, masks, deluges, SVG tracing, image painting, text painting\n- Configurable paper texture, edge darkening, granulation, evaporation\n- Optional open-edge boundary modes (paint can fall off the page)\n- Optional gravity bias (8-direction compass plus radial)\n- WebGL rendering when available; CPU fallback always works\n\n## Quick examples\n\n### Splash a deluge near a corner\n\n```js\nwc.splash([{ x: 100, y: 100, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\n### Animate an SVG drawing\n\n```js\nconst svg = await fetch('/my-logo.svg').then(r => r.text());\nawait wc.traceSVG(svg, { pigment: 'rose', speed: 600 });\n```\n\n### Open boundaries with radial gravity (paint falls off all edges)\n\n```js\nwc.edgeMode('gravity');\nwc.gravityDirection('radial');\nwc.gravityStrength(0.10);  // moderate pull\n```\n\n### Chained configuration\n\n```js\nwc.pigment('blue').brushSize(40).paperWetness('damp');\n```\n\n## TypeScript\n\nTypes ship with the package. No `@types/` install needed.\n\n```ts\nimport { Washes, type WashesInstance, type EdgeMode } from 'washes';\n\nconst wc: WashesInstance = Washes.create(host);\n\nconst mode: EdgeMode = 'gravity';\nwc.edgeMode(mode);\n```\n\n## Loading without a bundler\n\nThe package also attaches `window.Washes` for use via a `<script>` tag.\n\n```html\n<script src=\"node_modules/washes/src/washes.js\"></script>\n<script>\n  const wc = window.Washes.create(document.getElementById('host'));\n</script>\n```\n\n## Documentation\n\nThe full documentation \u2014 including the math and physics, debugging history, prior art, and \"Dig Deeper\" sections \u2014 lives in the playground HTML page that ships in `examples/`. Open `examples/playground.html` in a browser to explore.\n\n## License\n\nMIT. See [LICENSE](./LICENSE).\n\n## Citation\n\nIf you use Washes in academic or technical work, please cite via [CITATION.cff](./CITATION.cff) or the BibTeX below:\n\n```bibtex\n@software{washes,\n  title = {Washes: a JavaScript watercolor simulation},\n  author = {Stephanie},\n  year = {2026},\n  url = {https://github.com/yourusername/washes},\n  note = {Implementation of Curtis et al. 1997, with extensions}\n}\n```\n\n## Acknowledgements\n\nBuilt on the work of:\n- **Curtis, Anderson, Seims, Fleischer, Salesin** \u2014 *Computer-Generated Watercolor* (SIGGRAPH 1997). The paper this lib implements.\n- **Kubelka & Munk** \u2014 *Ein Beitrag zur Optik der Farbanstriche* (1931). The optical compositing model.\n- **Stam** \u2014 *Stable Fluids* (SIGGRAPH 1999). The semi-Lagrangian advection scheme.\n- **Foster & Metaxas** \u2014 *Realistic Animation of Liquids* (Graphical Models 1996). The shallow-water fluid step.\n- **Bridson** \u2014 *Fluid Simulation for Computer Graphics* (2nd ed., 2015). Reference for boundary conditions and divergence correction.\n- **Sochorov\u00e1 & Jamri\u0161ka** \u2014 *Practical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting* (Mixbox, SIGGRAPH Asia 2021). Sibling work in the same Kubelka\u2013Munk lineage.\n\nThe project was nerd-sniped into existence at the Recurse Center by Dan Knutson, Cyrene Zhang, and Kanad Gupta. Extended conversations with Evan Gedrich Pintado, Iris Fernandes Valdez, Lissa Hyacinth, and Jonathan King shaped its scope. See the in-app \"Origin\" section for the full story.\n",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"markdown\", \"code\": \"# Washes\\n\\nAn interactive watercolor simulation library for the browser. Built on Curtis et al.'s 1997 SIGGRAPH paper \\\"Computer-Generated Watercolor,\\\" with a three-layer fluid + pigment + paper model and Kubelka\\u2013Munk optical compositing.\\n\\n```bash\\nnpm install washes\\n```\\n\\n```js\\nimport { Washes } from 'washes';\\n\\nconst wc = Washes.create(document.getElementById('canvas-host'), {\\n  gouacheMode: 'auto',\\n});\\n\\nwc.splash([{ x: 200, y: 200, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n## What it does\\n\\n- Physics-based watercolor simulation running at 60fps in the browser\\n- Three pigments (quinacridone rose, hansa yellow, cerulean blue) composited via Kubelka\\u2013Munk\\n- Brushes, splashes, lifts, masks, deluges, SVG tracing, image painting, text painting\\n- Configurable paper texture, edge darkening, granulation, evaporation\\n- Optional open-edge boundary modes (paint can fall off the page)\\n- Optional gravity bias (8-direction compass plus radial)\\n- WebGL rendering when available; CPU fallback always works\\n\\n## Quick examples\\n\\n### Splash a deluge near a corner\\n\\n```js\\nwc.splash([{ x: 100, y: 100, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n### Animate an SVG drawing\\n\\n```js\\nconst svg = await fetch('/my-logo.svg').then(r => r.text());\\nawait wc.traceSVG(svg, { pigment: 'rose', speed: 600 });\\n```\\n\\n### Open boundaries with radial gravity (paint falls off all edges)\\n\\n```js\\nwc.edgeMode('gravity');\\nwc.gravityDirection('radial');\\nwc.gravityStrength(0.10);  // moderate pull\\n```\\n\\n### Chained configuration\\n\\n```js\\nwc.pigment('blue').brushSize(40).paperWetness('damp');\\n```\\n\\n## TypeScript\\n\\nTypes ship with the package. No `@types/` install needed.\\n\\n```ts\\nimport { Washes, type WashesInstance, type EdgeMode } from 'washes';\\n\\nconst wc: WashesInstance = Washes.create(host);\\n\\nconst mode: EdgeMode = 'gravity';\\nwc.edgeMode(mode);\\n```\\n\\n## Loading without a bundler\\n\\nThe package also attaches `window.Washes` for use via a `<script>` tag.\\n\\n```html\\n<script src=\\\"node_modules/washes/src/washes.js\\\"></script>\\n<script>\\n  const wc = window.Washes.create(document.getElementById('host'));\\n</script>\\n```\\n\\n## Documentation\\n\\nThe full documentation \\u2014 including the math and physics, debugging history, prior art, and \\\"Dig Deeper\\\" sections \\u2014 lives in the playground HTML page that ships in `examples/`. Open `examples/playground.html` in a browser to explore.\\n\\n## License\\n\\nMIT. See [LICENSE](./LICENSE).\\n\\n## Citation\\n\\nIf you use Washes in academic or technical work, please cite via [CITATION.cff](./CITATION.cff) or the BibTeX below:\\n\\n```bibtex\\n@software{washes,\\n  title = {Washes: a JavaScript watercolor simulation},\\n  author = {Stephanie},\\n  year = {2026},\\n  url = {https://github.com/yourusername/washes},\\n  note = {Implementation of Curtis et al. 1997, with extensions}\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Acknowledgements\\n\\nBuilt on the work of:\\n- **Curtis, Anderson, Seims, Fleischer, Salesin** \\u2014 *Computer-Generated Watercolor* (SIGGRAPH 1997). The paper this lib implements.\\n- **Kubelka & Munk** \\u2014 *Ein Beitrag zur Optik der Farbanstriche* (1931). The optical compositing model.\\n- **Stam** \\u2014 *Stable Fluids* (SIGGRAPH 1999). The semi-Lagrangian advection scheme.\\n- **Foster & Metaxas** \\u2014 *Realistic Animation of Liquids* (Graphical Models 1996). The shallow-water fluid step.\\n- **Bridson** \\u2014 *Fluid Simulation for Computer Graphics* (2nd ed., 2015). Reference for boundary conditions and divergence correction.\\n- **Sochorov\\u00e1 & Jamri\\u0161ka** \\u2014 *Practical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting* (Mixbox, SIGGRAPH Asia 2021). Sibling work in the same Kubelka\\u2013Munk lineage.\\n\\nThe project was nerd-sniped into existence at the Recurse Center by Dan Knutson, Cyrene Zhang, and Kanad Gupta. Extended conversations with Evan Gedrich Pintado, Iris Fernandes Valdez, Lissa Hyacinth, and Jonathan King shaped its scope. See the in-app \\\"Origin\\\" section for the full story.\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/washes-pkg/README.md\"}"

My initial README example claimed `wc.pigment('blue').brushSize(40).paperWetness('damp')` would work \u2014 it wouldn't have at runtime. I caught this when the TypeScript smoke test errored, fixed both the README and the types to match how the lib actually behaves. **The TS types are now stricter than my prose was**, which is the right direction.\n\n**ESM + global, not just ESM.** The lib still attaches to `window.Washes` for script-tag use cases. The package's `package.json` declares it as an ESM module (`\"type\": \"module\"`) so modern bundlers do the right thing, but the runtime still works in legacy contexts.\n\n**Lib file untouched except for the tail.** I didn't refactor anything internal \u2014 I only added an ESM export block and a `globalThis` polyfill at the bottom of the IIFE. The simulation behavior is identical.\n\n**Author name + GitHub URL are placeholders.** Edit `package.json`, `LICENSE`, and `CITATION.cff` before publishing or pushing to GitHub.\n\n## To actually publish\n\n```bash\ncd washes-pkg\n# Edit the placeholder author / repo URL in package.json, LICENSE, CITATION.cff\nnpm login\nnpm publish --dry-run    # verify what would be published\nnpm publish              # for real\n```\n\nThe `--dry-run` will show you the exact file list shipped to npm \u2014 `.npmignore` excludes the 912KB playground HTML, so the published package is ~30KB (lib + types + README + license).\n\nIf the name `washes` turns out to be taken when you `npm publish`, scope it: `\"@yourusername/washes\"` in `package.json`, then `npm publish --access public`.",

The new canvas element didn't match the old <code>#canvas</code> selector, so it fell back to the browser default cursor for <code>&lt;canvas&gt;</code> elements \u2014 which historically is <code>crosshair</code>. The crosshair (a thin plus sign with a dot at the center) was visible through transparent brush-preview rings, looking like a stray artifact inside the cursor circle. Most visible with the rainbow brush whose preview is just a hollow ring. Fixed by retargeting the rule at <code>#canvas-host canvas</code>, and as belt-and-suspenders the lib itself now writes <code>cursor:none</code> directly onto its canvas element so the artifact can't recur even if surrounding CSS drifts. The deluge-mode crosshair override (intentional crosshair when click-to-deluge is armed) got the same retarget.</li>\\n/home/claude/watercolor.html:7439:          <li><span class=\\\"cl-tag cl-fix\\\">fix</span> <strong>Cursor preview was invisible on desktop.</strong> The <code>.cursor-preview</code> element had <code>display: none</code> as its default CSS &mdash; but opacity transitions don't work from <code>display: none</code> (the browser short-circuits before computing opacity). So the cursor was never visible regardless of mobile-mode state. The wiring's pointer-move handler was setting <code>opacity = '1'</code> on a hidden element, which did nothing. Fix: when cursor preview is enabled, the wiring now sets <code>display: 'block'</code>, allowing opacity to transition. Initial opacity stays 0 until first pointer movement so the element doesn't flash on page load.</li>\\n/home/claude/watercolor.html:7447:          <li>Refactor: the wiring's cursor-preview block now has two flags &mdash; <code>_cursorVisible</code> (current opacity state) and <code>_cursorEnabled</code> (allowed to render at all). The mobile-mode UI handler now routes through <code>wc.cursorPreview(!on)</code> instead of touching the element directly, so the lib's flag and the wiring's element stay in sync no matter which side of the API the flip comes from.</li>\\n/home/claude/watercolor.html:7562:          <li><span class=\\\"cl-tag cl-fix\\\">fix</span> Cursor preview TDZ error: <code>wireSlider</code> fires an initial input event to sync the readout, which called <code>updateCursorPreview()</code>, which referenced <code>cursorEl</code> &mdash; declared later in the script. So the cursor was never visible regardless of mobile-mode state. The wiring's pointer-move handler was setting <code>opacity = '1'</code> on a hidden element, which did nothing.     "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add wetness heatmap renderer + helper before main render",
      "new_str": "// v0.86 \u2014 Wetness heatmap renderer. Creates (lazily) an overlay\n// canvas matching the main canvas dimensions, then maps wet[] through\n// a two-color gradient and writes the result to that overlay.\n// Compositing is via the browser (overlay sits above main canvas at\n// z-index 10), not via the lib's own pixel pipeline, so toggling on\n// and off is cheap and the heatmap doesn't affect the simulation.\nfunction _renderWetnessHeatmap() {\n  if (!_wetnessHeatmapEnabled) {\n    if (_wetnessOverlayCanvas) _wetnessOverlayCanvas.style.opacity = '0';\n    return;\n  }\n  // Lazy-create the overlay canvas the first time the heatmap is\n  // shown. Sized in display pixels, same as the main canvas.\n  if (!_wetnessOverlayCanvas) {\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas = document.createElement('canvas');\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas.width  = canvas.width;\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas.height = canvas.height;\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas.style.cssText =\n      'display:block;position:absolute;inset:0;width:100%;height:100%;' +\n      'pointer-events:none;z-index:10;opacity:0;' +\n      'transition:opacity 0.18s ease-out;';\n    targetEl.appendChild(_wetnessOverlayCanvas);\n    _wetnessOverlayCtx = _wetnessOverlayCanvas.getContext('2d');\n  }\n  // Resize overlay if canvas was resized.\n  if (_wetnessOverlayCanvas.width !== canvas.width ||\n      _wetnessOverlayCanvas.height !== canvas.height) {\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas.width  = canvas.width;\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas.height = canvas.height;\n    _wetnessOverlayImageData = null;\n  }\n  // The simulation grid is smaller than the display canvas. Render\n  // the heatmap at grid resolution into a small ImageData, then let\n  // the browser's drawImage upsample. This is much faster than\n  // touching every display pixel.\n  if (!_wetnessOverlayImageData || _wetnessOverlayImageData.width !== GW) {\n    _wetnessOverlayImageData = _wetnessOverlayCtx.createImageData(GW, GH);\n  }\n  const dst = _wetnessOverlayImageData.data;\n  const r0 = _wetnessLowColor[0],  g0 = _wetnessLowColor[1],  b0 = _wetnessLowColor[2];\n  const r1 = _wetnessHighColor[0], g1 = _wetnessHighColor[1], b1 = _wetnessHighColor[2];\n  // wet[] is 0..~1.5. Normalize against MAX_WET so saturated cells\n  // hit the full high-color. Below MASK_THRESHOLD render fully\n  // transparent (no overlay over genuinely dry paper, so the user\n  // can see the painting underneath the gradient).\n  const MAX_WET = 1.0;\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    const w = wet[i];\n    const di = i * 4;\n    if (w < MASK_THRESHOLD) {\n      dst[di + 3] = 0;\n      continue;\n    }\n    let t = w / MAX_WET;\n    if (t > 1) t = 1;\n    dst[di    ] = (r0 + (r1 - r0) * t) | 0;\n    dst[di + 1] = (g0 + (g1 - g0) * t) | 0;\n    dst[di + 2] = (b0 + (b1 - b0) * t) | 0;\n    dst[di + 3] = 160;  // ~63% alpha so painting stays visible underneath\n  }\n  // Upload the grid-resolution ImageData to a tiny offscreen canvas\n  // so we can use drawImage's bilinear upsampling to fill the\n  // full-size overlay. Putting ImageData directly is 1:1 pixel\n  // (would render the overlay at grid resolution scaled by CSS,\n  // which looks blocky on retina).\n  if (!_wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas) {\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas = document.createElement('canvas');\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas.width = GW;\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas.height = GH;\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCtx = _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas.getContext('2d');\n  }\n  if (_wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas.width !== GW) {\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas.width = GW;\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas.height = GH;\n  }\n  _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCtx.putImageData(_wetnessOverlayImageData, 0, 0);\n  _wetnessOverlayCtx.clearRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height);\n  _wetnessOverlayCtx.imageSmoothingEnabled = true;\n  _wetnessOverlayCtx.drawImage(\n    _wetnessOverlayCanvas._tinyCanvas,\n    0, 0, GW, GH,\n    0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height\n  );\n  _wetnessOverlayCanvas.style.opacity = '1';\n}\n\n// pixels outside the active rect \u2014 currently just paper color change.",
      "old_str": "// pixels outside the active rect \u2014 currently just paper color change.",
      "path": "/home/claude/washes-pkg/src/washes.js"

Dry areas\n          stay transparent so the painting underneath is still\n          visible. Useful for staging wet-on-wet layering &mdash;\n          you can see exactly where new paint will bleed vs where\n          it'll dry as a hard edge.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Default stops are pale yellow (dry, <code>#FCF3A7</code>)\n          to deep blue (wet, <code>#296FA7</code>). Both pickers in\n          the UI (under the new \"Heatmap stops\" row); changes apply\n          live. Public API:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.wetnessHeatmap()                        // returns boolean\nwc.wetnessHeatmap(true)                    // enable, default colors\nwc.wetnessHeatmap(true, '#fcf3a7', '#296fa7')  // enable with colors\nwc.wetnessHeatmap({ enabled: true, low: '#abc', high: '#def' })</pre>\n        <p>\n          Colors accept <code>'#rgb'</code>, <code>'#rrggbb'</code>,\n          or <code>[r, g, b]</code> 0..255 arrays.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Implementation: a separate overlay canvas is appended to\n          the host element with z-index above the main canvas.\n          Wetness data is rendered at simulation-grid resolution\n          (much smaller than display resolution) into a tiny offscreen\n          canvas, then upsampled via the browser's bilinear\n          <code>drawImage()</code>. This is much faster than touching\n          every display pixel and gives a smooth gradient that scales\n          cleanly to retina displays. The overlay is created lazily\n          on first enable, so disabled-mode users pay no cost.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Deluge cursor</h3>\n        <p>\n          When click-to-deluge mode is armed, the brush preview now\n          shows a target reticle (three concentric rings + center\n          dot) instead of the regular brush color. Previously the\n          cursor still showed the last-used brush color, which made\n          it hard to tell at a glance whether the next click would\n          stamp paint or trigger a deluge.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Pure CSS \u2014 no JS changes. <code>::before</code> /\n          <code>::after</code> pseudo-elements on the cursor preview\n          render the outer and middle rings; a nested\n          <code>.deluge-target</code> element renders the center dot\n          (only shown when the body has the <code>.ws-deluge-mode</code>\n          class). The system <code>crosshair</code> cursor that was\n          previously also shown is now hidden &mdash; the custom CSS\n          indicator is sufficient.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Full-width dryness bar</h3>\n        <p>\n          The dryness progress bar at the top of the viewport and the\n          symmetric pigment bar at the bottom were positioned\n          <code>absolute</code>, which bounded them to the nearest\n          positioned ancestor instead of the full viewport. With the\n          docs panel open, both bars stopped short of the right edge.\n          Changed to <code>position: fixed</code> so they always span\n          the full viewport regardless of containing layout.\n        </p>\n\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\nprint('docs updated')\nPY\n\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.85-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.86-wiring.js\nsed -i 's/v0\\.85-wiring/v0.86-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.86-wiring.js\n\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\nlines = src.splitlines()\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\/script>')\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-lib.js').read())\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.86-wiring.js').read())\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if '<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>' in line:\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>', '<div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>')\n        break\nscript_start = None;     "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.86 with the three coding items (1, 2, 3). Now the non-coding answers:\n\n---\n\n## #4 \u2014 Acrylic paint, conceptually\n\nYes, absolutely possible \u2014 and worth thinking about because it's different from watercolor in interesting ways.\n\nThe defining differences between acrylic and watercolor (the lib's current model):\n\n1. **Opacity, not transparency.** Acrylic is fundamentally opaque. Where watercolor needs Kubelka\u2013Munk's two-flux radiative-transfer math because light passes *through* the pigment layer and bounces off the paper, acrylic blocks light at the layer itself. The optical model collapses from K\u2013M to something much simpler: the layer color *is* the final color, modulo edge antialiasing.\n\n2. **No bleeding, no granulation.** Acrylic dries fast and doesn't migrate through paper fibers. The shallow-water fluid layer (`u`, `v`, pressure) is mostly irrelevant \u2014 strokes stay where you put them. The lib's whole \"wet edges flow into each other\" behavior would need to be disabled or radically constrained.\n\n3. **Color *mixes* differently.** Two wet acrylic colors physically mix to a new color (CMY-like subtractive mixing in oil/binder). Two wet watercolors stay layered (Kubelka\u2013Munk's strength). Mixbox (Sochorov\u00e1 & Jamri\u0161ka 2021, already cited in your docs) explicitly handles this \u2014 it was designed for digital painting tools, not for fluid simulation. **Mixbox is basically the right model for an acrylic mode.**\n\n4. **Drying matters less for mixing, more for layering.** Acrylic over wet acrylic = mixing. Acrylic over dry acrylic = pure overpainting, no mixing at all. The simulation's \"wet\"/\"dry\" distinction is binary in a way it isn't for watercolor (where even dry pigment can be reactivated by water).\n\n5. **Brush behavior is texturally different.** Heavy-body acrylic has visible brush strokes (impasto) that hold their shape; watercolor brush marks flatten into the wet medium. To simulate impasto you'd need a *height field* on top of the current model \u2014 pigment thickness rendered as 3D relief via faux-lighting (like in some Procreate brush packs).\n\n**What an \"acrylic mode\" for Washes would actually look like**, concretely:\n\n- Replace K-M compositing with Mixbox's RGB-in-RGB-out approximation (which is published and could legitimately be ported)\n- Disable or dramatically reduce flow/bleed (multiply `flow`, `paintLoad`, `waterLoad` by ~0.1)\n- Disable evaporation as the \"drying\" mechanism; instead, mark cells as dry the moment paint touches them above some threshold\n- Optional: add a height-field render pass for impasto texture using SSAO-style edge highlighting\n\nIt's not a small project \u2014 probably comparable in scope to the v0.81-0.84 boundary work. But it's a coherent feature that would give the lib a second medium without abandoning the architecture. If you ever want to do it, Mixbox is the right starting point and \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 / Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka have been generally welcoming about citation and reuse.\n\nFor a *quick* approximation right now: enable Gouache mode + set evaporation high + reduce flow + reduce paint load. You won't get true acrylic mixing physics but you'll get an opaque, fast-drying, low-bleed paint that *reads* as acrylic to a casual viewer.\n\n---\n\n## #5 \u2014 Best place to upload sketches for algorithm suggestions\n\nTwo options, both work for what you're describing:\n\n**Claude.ai chat (this interface or a fresh one)**: drag-and-drop image upload is built in. Paste a sketch or screenshot; I can look at it directly and suggest API call sequences. This is probably what you want for one-off sketches \u2014 you don't need a persistent project setup.\n\n**Claude.ai Projects**: if you anticipate doing this multiple times against the same library, create a Project, attach `src/washes.js` and `src/washes.d.ts` as project files, and then each sketch you upload there will get suggestions that reference the actual API you've built. The project remembers context across sessions so I don't have to re-learn the lib each time.\n\n**Claude Code**: this is the right tool if you want to iterate \u2014 upload sketch, get code, run it locally against the lib, refine. It's set up for the loop of \"look at file, edit file, test, repeat\" in a way chat isn't. Especially good if you have multiple sketches \u2192 multiple compositions and want them all in a single repo.\n\n**The workflow I'd actually recommend:**\n\n1. **Create a Claude.ai Project** called \"Washes sketches\" (or similar)\n2. **Attach as project knowledge:**\n   - `src/washes.js` (the lib)\n   - `src/washes.d.ts` (the types \u2014 helps me suggest correct method signatures)\n   - The `README.md`\n   - A short \"compositions cookbook\" doc with 3-5 example call sequences you've already used (deluge presets you like, color combinations, etc.) so I have a reference style\n3. **In each new chat in that project**, upload a sketch and say something like \"suggest a Washes call sequence that recreates this composition.\"\n4. **When you're ready to implement**, take the suggested code into Claude Code (or your editor) and iterate against the running playground.\n\nThe reason this works better than ad-hoc chat: project knowledge means I have the actual API surface in context, so I can suggest valid method calls instead of approximations. Without the lib in context, I'd suggest things like `wc.brush({ color: 'red' })` which doesn't exist.\n\nIf you want, I can draft the \"compositions cookbook\" doc as a starter \u2014 a few canonical examples (radial gravity background, centered deluge, SVG trace transition) that would give me a style reference to imitate when suggesting code for your sketches. Just say the word.",

    "id": "toolu_01BnCUxTERMreamB52AhBLYJ",
    "name": "create_file",
    "input": {
      "description": "Compositions cookbook \u2014 call sequences for common Washes results, structured as project-knowledge reference",
      "file_text": "# Washes compositions cookbook\n\nA reference of working call sequences for the Washes library, organized by visual outcome rather than by API method. The goal is to give Claude (and you) a style guide: when suggesting code for a new sketch, the suggestions should look and read like the recipes below.\n\nEvery recipe assumes you've already done:\n\n```js\nimport { Washes } from 'washes';\nconst wc = Washes.create(document.getElementById('host'), { gouacheMode: 'auto' });\n```\n\nRecipes are organized from atomic (one-shot effects) to compositional (multi-step pieces). Each one names what it produces and the API methods it relies on.\n\n---\n\n## Atomic effects\n\n### A simple deluge\n\nThe fundamental building block. One radial outburst of pigment at a click position.\n\n```js\nwc.splash([{ x: 400, y: 300, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\nVariations:\n- Higher `velocity` (60-80): more violent burst, takes longer to settle.\n- Lower `velocity` (10-20): gentle ripple, settles quickly.\n- Multiple epicenters in one call: simultaneous bursts that interact.\n\n```js\n// Three simultaneous deluges in a triangle\nwc.splash([\n  { x: 300, y: 200, velocity: 30 },\n  { x: 500, y: 200, velocity: 30 },\n  { x: 400, y: 400, velocity: 30 },\n], 'deluge');\n```\n\n### A solid brush stroke\n\n```js\nwc.pigment('blue');\nwc.brushSize(40);\n// Then either: paint via pointer events (already wired by default),\n// OR programmatically stamp:\nwc.paintAt(200, 150, 20, 'blue', 0.8);  // grid coords, gridRadius, pigment, strength\n```\n\nNote: `paintAt` uses grid coordinates, not display pixels. Use `wc.toGrid(x, y)` to convert if you have display coords.\n\n### Lift (erase)\n\n```js\nwc.pigment('lift');\nwc.brushSize(60);\n// Paint with the lift brush \u2014 removes pigment from cells it touches.\n// Returns the active brush to lift mode until you switch back:\nwc.pigment(0);  // back to rose\n```\n\n---\n\n## Backgrounds\n\n### Even wash background\n\nCover the entire canvas with a single pigment at low load. Good as an underpainting layer for portfolio backgrounds.\n\n```js\nconst w = wc.state().DISPLAY_W ?? 1080;\nconst h = wc.state().DISPLAY_H ?? 900;\n\nwc.pigment('yellow');\nwc.brushSize(800);\nwc.paintLoad(0.15);\nwc.splash([{ x: w / 2, y: h / 2, velocity: 5 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\nLower `paintLoad` for a more transparent wash; raise for a more opaque tint.\n\n### Three-pigment Venn background\n\nA subtle compositional background using all three pigments at low load. Each pigment becomes a soft circle; intersections mix via Kubelka\u2013Munk. See the v0.81+ \"Venn preview\" in the docs for what each intersection looks like at the chosen load.\n\n```js\nconst w = 1080, h = 900;\n\nwc.brushSize(600);\nwc.paintLoad(0.10);\n\nwc.pigment('rose');\nwc.splash([{ x: w * 0.40, y: h * 0.45, velocity: 8 }], 'deluge');\n\nwc.pigment('yellow');\nwc.splash([{ x: w * 0.60, y: h * 0.45, velocity: 8 }], 'deluge');\n\nwc.pigment('blue');\nwc.splash([{ x: w * 0.50, y: h * 0.65, velocity: 8 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\n### Radial-gravity ambient\n\nPigment drifts continuously toward all edges. Good for moody, never-twice-the-same backgrounds. The wash never fully settles.\n\n```js\nwc.edgeMode('gravity');\nwc.gravityDirection('radial');\nwc.gravityStrength(0.08);   // gentle pull; raise for more dramatic\n\n// Drop a single centered splash that will radiate outward forever\nwc.splash([{ x: 540, y: 450, velocity: 30 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\n---\n\n## Transitions (portfolio in/out)\n\n### \"Drawing in\" \u2014 animate an SVG over time\n\nFor a logo or shape that appears as the page loads. The SVG paths are drawn as if by hand at the given speed.\n\n```js\nconst svg = await fetch('/logo.svg').then(r => r.text());\n\nawait wc.traceSVG(svg, {\n  pigment: 'blue',\n  brushSize: 32,\n  speed: 600,            // px/sec\n  scale: 0.8,\n  translateX: 100,\n  translateY: 50,\n});\n```\n\nThe returned Promise resolves when the trace completes. Chain another effect after:\n\n```js\nawait wc.traceSVG(svg, { pigment: 'blue', speed: 600 });\n// After draw-in, optionally bloom the lines outward:\nwc.splash([{ x: 540, y: 450, velocity: 20 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\n### \"Falling off\" \u2014 open edges + gravity-down\n\nFor a page-exit transition. Paint slides off the bottom edge as the user navigates away.\n\n```js\nwc.edgeMode('gravity');\nwc.gravityDirection('down');\nwc.gravityStrength(0.20);   // strong pull\nwc.velocityClamp(3.0);      // raise the cap so flow is dramatic\n// The simulation does the rest. After ~3 seconds the canvas should\n// be mostly empty.\n```\n\n### \"Obliterate\" \u2014 built-in disintegration\n\nThe lib has a built-in obliterate animation that progressively destroys the painting. Good for dramatic exits.\n\n```js\nawait wc.obliterate({\n  duration: 1200,         // ms\n  easing: 'ease-out',\n});\n```\n\n---\n\n## Compositional pieces (multi-step)\n\n### \"Sunset with bleed\"\n\nVertical color gradient: rose at top, yellow in middle, blue at bottom. Use small splashes at different y positions to build up the gradient.\n\n```js\nconst w = 1080;\nwc.brushSize(900);\nwc.paintLoad(0.12);\n\nwc.pigment('rose');\nfor (let x = 0; x <= w; x += 150) {\n  wc.splash([{ x, y: 100, velocity: 6 }], 'deluge');\n}\n\nwc.pigment('yellow');\nfor (let x = 0; x <= w; x += 150) {\n  wc.splash([{ x, y: 350, velocity: 6 }], 'deluge');\n}\n\nwc.pigment('blue');\nfor (let x = 0; x <= w; x += 150) {\n  wc.splash([{ x, y: 600, velocity: 6 }], 'deluge');\n}\n```\n\n### \"Constellation\" \u2014 sparse points of single-pigment color\n\nA scatter of small splashes at semi-random positions. Use as a background or in the corners of a hero image.\n\n```js\nconst W = 1080, H = 900;\nconst points = 12;\nwc.brushSize(50);\nwc.pigment('rose');\n\nconst splashes = [];\nfor (let i = 0; i < points; i++) {\n  splashes.push({\n    x: 100 + Math.random() * (W - 200),\n    y: 100 + Math.random() * (H - 200),\n    velocity: 8 + Math.random() * 8,\n  });\n}\nwc.splash(splashes, 'splash');\n```\n\nFor Poisson-disk-like spacing (more even-looking placement), generate candidate points and reject any too close to an existing point.\n\n### \"Stained edge frame\"\n\nA subtle wash that hugs the edges of the canvas, leaving the center clear. Used as a frame around content.\n\n```js\nconst W = 1080, H = 900;\n\nwc.brushSize(600);\nwc.paintLoad(0.08);\nwc.pigment('yellow');\n\n// Four corner splashes\nwc.splash([\n  { x: 0, y: 0, velocity: 5 },\n  { x: W, y: 0, velocity: 5 },\n  { x: 0, y: H, velocity: 5 },\n  { x: W, y: H, velocity: 5 },\n], 'deluge');\n```\n\nThe splashes radiate outward; mass conservation pushes pigment toward the canvas corners. With `edgeMode: 'open'` the corners would drain, breaking the effect \u2014 keep closed mode for this one.\n\n### \"Wet stage for portrait\"\n\nA pre-wet area in the middle of the canvas where you'll later paint a portrait or other subject. The pre-wetting makes subsequent paint bleed more, giving a soft \"rendered in watercolor\" look to anything painted there.\n\n```js\nconst cx = 540, cy = 450;\n\n// First: pre-wet the area without much pigment\nwc.pigment('water');\nwc.brushSize(400);\nwc.paintLoad(0.05);\nwc.splash([{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 4 }], 'deluge');\n\n// Wait briefly for the water to spread\nawait new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500));\n\n// Now paint into the wet area \u2014 it'll bleed naturally\nwc.pigment('rose');\nwc.brushSize(40);\nwc.paintAt(...wc.toGrid(cx - 30, cy - 30).map(Math.round), 15, 'rose', 0.7);\nwc.paintAt(...wc.toGrid(cx + 30, cy + 30).map(Math.round), 15, 'rose', 0.7);\n```\n\nThe pre-wet stage acts like a real watercolor \"wet-on-wet\" technique.\n\n### \"Color story\" \u2014 palette demo for a portfolio piece\n\nDrop three swatches of equal size at the three thirds of the canvas. Useful as a portfolio piece showing off the lib's color compositing.\n\n```js\nconst W = 1080, H = 900;\nconst y = H * 0.5;\n\nwc.brushSize(500);\nwc.paintLoad(0.18);\n\nwc.pigment('rose');\nwc.splash([{ x: W * 0.25, y, velocity: 10 }], 'deluge');\n\nwc.pigment('yellow');\nwc.splash([{ x: W * 0.50, y, velocity: 10 }], 'deluge');\n\nwc.pigment('blue');\nwc.splash([{ x: W * 0.75, y, velocity: 10 }], 'deluge');\n```\n\n---\n\n## Style reference (when suggesting code for a new sketch)\n\nA good Washes call sequence has these properties:\n\n1. **Coordinates are display pixels** (not grid coords) unless using `paintAt`. Use `wc.toGrid(x, y)` if grid coords are needed.\n2. **`brushSize` is in display pixels.** Default 28, range realistically 4-2000.\n3. **`paintLoad` and similar are in 0..1.** Higher = more opaque.\n4. **Splash `velocity` is roughly 5-80.** Below 5 is gentle; above 60 is dramatic.\n5. **Multi-pigment compositions** set the pigment before each operation. Don't try to \"mix\" pigments mid-call \u2014 the lib's Kubelka\u2013Munk compositing does that automatically when overlapping pigments land at the same grid cell.\n6. **Don't await `splash`** \u2014 it's synchronous. Do `await` `traceSVG`, `paintText`, `paintImage`, `obliterate`. They return Promises.\n7. **Match the deluge style to the mood**: `'deluge'` for strong outward radiation, `'splash'` for sharper localized splash, `'spray'` for diffuse coverage.\n8. **Backgrounds want low `paintLoad` (0.08\u20130.15) and big brushes (400\u2013900).** Foreground subjects want default `paintLoad` (0.5+) and small brushes (20\u201360).\n9. **Use `edgeMode: 'gravity'` + `direction: 'radial'`** for ambient, alive-feeling backgrounds. Use `edgeMode: 'closed'` (default) when you want a specific composition to settle into its final form.\n10. **Code-style preference**: terse method chains where possible (`wc.pigment('blue')`), one effect per statement, comments explaining *why* not *what*.\n\n---\n\n## Methods worth knowing about for sketch-to-code translation\n\nWhen the sketch shows... | Reach for...\n--- | ---\nOutward radial burst, dramatic | `splash([...], 'deluge', { velocity: 40+ })`\nSingle localized splat | `splash([...], 'splash', { velocity: 20-30 })`\nDiffuse, soft, multiple points | `splash([many], 'spray', { velocity: 5-10 })`\nAnimated line drawing | `traceSVG(...)` (returns Promise)\nCover large area uniformly | `brushSize(800)` + `paintLoad(0.1)` + `splash(...)`\nText appearing | `paintText(text, { fontSize, x, y, pigment })` (Promise)\nImage stenciled in watercolor | `paintImage(url, { threshold: 0.5 })` (Promise)\nEmpty masked region (clear corner) | `pigment('mask')` then paint where you want frozen\nPre-wet stage for bleed | `pigment('water')` + `paintLoad(0.05)` then splash\nErase | `pigment('lift')` + brush over area\nPage-exit animation | `edgeMode('gravity')` + `gravityStrength(0.2)`, OR `obliterate({ duration })`\nAmbient never-settles background | `edgeMode('gravity')` + `gravityDirection('radial')` + `gravityStrength(0.05-0.1)`\nVisualize wetness | `wetnessHeatmap(true, lowColor, highColor)`\n\n---\n\n## Tone\n\nWhen suggesting code from a sketch, prefer:\n\n- **3-10 lines of code, heavily commented.** A sketch isn't a full application \u2014 the recipe should be readable in 30 seconds.\n- **Variables for any numbers a user might want to tweak** (canvas dimensions, position offsets, color choices). Not just inline magic numbers.\n- **An \"if you want X\" follow-up suggesting one variation.** \"If you want the bleed to be more aggressive, raise `paintLoad` to 0.25.\"\n- **Acknowledge what the sketch can't capture in code.** Watercolor has randomness; the rendered output will look different each time. If the sketch shows specific drip shapes, those will vary; if it shows specific colors, those are achievable.\n\nWhen the sketch shows something the lib can't do (true wet-on-dry overpainting without bleed, impasto texture, drying *patterns* like cauliflowering), say so directly and suggest the closest available technique.\n",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"markdown\", \"code\": \"# Washes compositions cookbook\\n\\nA reference of working call sequences for the Washes library, organized by visual outcome rather than by API method. The goal is to give Claude (and you) a style guide: when suggesting code for a new sketch, the suggestions should look and read like the recipes below.\\n\\nEvery recipe assumes you've already done:\\n\\n```js\\nimport { Washes } from 'washes';\\nconst wc = Washes.create(document.getElementById('host'), { gouacheMode: 'auto' });\\n```\\n\\nRecipes are organized from atomic (one-shot effects) to compositional (multi-step pieces). Each one names what it produces and the API methods it relies on.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Atomic effects\\n\\n### A simple deluge\\n\\nThe fundamental building block. One radial outburst of pigment at a click position.\\n\\n```js\\nwc.splash([{ x: 400, y: 300, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\nVariations:\\n- Higher `velocity` (60-80): more violent burst, takes longer to settle.\\n- Lower `velocity` (10-20): gentle ripple, settles quickly.\\n- Multiple epicenters in one call: simultaneous bursts that interact.\\n\\n```js\\n// Three simultaneous deluges in a triangle\\nwc.splash([\\n  { x: 300, y: 200, velocity: 30 },\\n  { x: 500, y: 200, velocity: 30 },\\n  { x: 400, y: 400, velocity: 30 },\\n], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n### A solid brush stroke\\n\\n```js\\nwc.pigment('blue');\\nwc.brushSize(40);\\n// Then either: paint via pointer events (already wired by default),\\n// OR programmatically stamp:\\nwc.paintAt(200, 150, 20, 'blue', 0.8);  // grid coords, gridRadius, pigment, strength\\n```\\n\\nNote: `paintAt` uses grid coordinates, not display pixels. Use `wc.toGrid(x, y)` to convert if you have display coords.\\n\\n### Lift (erase)\\n\\n```js\\nwc.pigment('lift');\\nwc.brushSize(60);\\n// Paint with the lift brush \\u2014 removes pigment from cells it touches.\\n// Returns the active brush to lift mode until you switch back:\\nwc.pigment(0);  // back to rose\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Backgrounds\\n\\n### Even wash background\\n\\nCover the entire canvas with a single pigment at low load. Good as an underpainting layer for portfolio backgrounds.\\n\\n```js\\nconst w = wc.state().DISPLAY_W ?? 1080;\\nconst h = wc.state().DISPLAY_H ?? 900;\\n\\nwc.pigment('yellow');\\nwc.brushSize(800);\\nwc.paintLoad(0.15);\\nwc.splash([{ x: w / 2, y: h / 2, velocity: 5 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\nLower `paintLoad` for a more transparent wash; raise for a more opaque tint.\\n\\n### Three-pigment Venn background\\n\\nA subtle compositional background using all three pigments at low load. Each pigment becomes a soft circle; intersections mix via Kubelka\\u2013Munk. See the v0.81+ \\\"Venn preview\\\" in the docs for what each intersection looks like at the chosen load.\\n\\n```js\\nconst w = 1080, h = 900;\\n\\nwc.brushSize(600);\\nwc.paintLoad(0.10);\\n\\nwc.pigment('rose');\\nwc.splash([{ x: w * 0.40, y: h * 0.45, velocity: 8 }], 'deluge');\\n\\nwc.pigment('yellow');\\nwc.splash([{ x: w * 0.60, y: h * 0.45, velocity: 8 }], 'deluge');\\n\\nwc.pigment('blue');\\nwc.splash([{ x: w * 0.50, y: h * 0.65, velocity: 8 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n### Radial-gravity ambient\\n\\nPigment drifts continuously toward all edges. Good for moody, never-twice-the-same backgrounds. The wash never fully settles.\\n\\n```js\\nwc.edgeMode('gravity');\\nwc.gravityDirection('radial');\\nwc.gravityStrength(0.08);   // gentle pull; raise for more dramatic\\n\\n// Drop a single centered splash that will radiate outward forever\\nwc.splash([{ x: 540, y: 450, velocity: 30 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Transitions (portfolio in/out)\\n\\n### \\\"Drawing in\\\" \\u2014 animate an SVG over time\\n\\nFor a logo or shape that appears as the page loads. The SVG paths are drawn as if by hand at the given speed.\\n\\n```js\\nconst svg = await fetch('/logo.svg').then(r => r.text());\\n\\nawait wc.traceSVG(svg, {\\n  pigment: 'blue',\\n  brushSize: 32,\\n  speed: 600,            // px/sec\\n  scale: 0.8,\\n  translateX: 100,\\n  translateY: 50,\\n});\\n```\\n\\nThe returned Promise resolves when the trace completes. Chain another effect after:\\n\\n```js\\nawait wc.traceSVG(svg, { pigment: 'blue', speed: 600 });\\n// After draw-in, optionally bloom the lines outward:\\nwc.splash([{ x: 540, y: 450, velocity: 20 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n### \\\"Falling off\\\" \\u2014 open edges + gravity-down\\n\\nFor a page-exit transition. Paint slides off the bottom edge as the user navigates away.\\n\\n```js\\nwc.edgeMode('gravity');\\nwc.gravityDirection('down');\\nwc.gravityStrength(0.20);   // strong pull\\nwc.velocityClamp(3.0);      // raise the cap so flow is dramatic\\n// The simulation does the rest. After ~3 seconds the canvas should\\n// be mostly empty.\\n```\\n\\n### \\\"Obliterate\\\" \\u2014 built-in disintegration\\n\\nThe lib has a built-in obliterate animation that progressively destroys the painting. Good for dramatic exits.\\n\\n```js\\nawait wc.obliterate({\\n  duration: 1200,         // ms\\n  easing: 'ease-out',\\n});\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Compositional pieces (multi-step)\\n\\n### \\\"Sunset with bleed\\\"\\n\\nVertical color gradient: rose at top, yellow in middle, blue at bottom. Use small splashes at different y positions to build up the gradient.\\n\\n```js\\nconst w = 1080;\\nwc.brushSize(900);\\nwc.paintLoad(0.12);\\n\\nwc.pigment('rose');\\nfor (let x = 0; x <= w; x += 150) {\\n  wc.splash([{ x, y: 100, velocity: 6 }], 'deluge');\\n}\\n\\nwc.pigment('yellow');\\nfor (let x = 0; x <= w; x += 150) {\\n  wc.splash([{ x, y: 350, velocity: 6 }], 'deluge');\\n}\\n\\nwc.pigment('blue');\\nfor (let x = 0; x <= w; x += 150) {\\n  wc.splash([{ x, y: 600, velocity: 6 }], 'deluge');\\n}\\n```\\n\\n### \\\"Constellation\\\" \\u2014 sparse points of single-pigment color\\n\\nA scatter of small splashes at semi-random positions. Use as a background or in the corners of a hero image.\\n\\n```js\\nconst W = 1080, H = 900;\\nconst points = 12;\\nwc.brushSize(50);\\nwc.pigment('rose');\\n\\nconst splashes = [];\\nfor (let i = 0; i < points; i++) {\\n  splashes.push({\\n    x: 100 + Math.random() * (W - 200),\\n    y: 100 + Math.random() * (H - 200),\\n    velocity: 8 + Math.random() * 8,\\n  });\\n}\\nwc.splash(splashes, 'splash');\\n```\\n\\nFor Poisson-disk-like spacing (more even-looking placement), generate candidate points and reject any too close to an existing point.\\n\\n### \\\"Stained edge frame\\\"\\n\\nA subtle wash that hugs the edges of the canvas, leaving the center clear. Used as a frame around content.\\n\\n```js\\nconst W = 1080, H = 900;\\n\\nwc.brushSize(600);\\nwc.paintLoad(0.08);\\nwc.pigment('yellow');\\n\\n// Four corner splashes\\nwc.splash([\\n  { x: 0, y: 0, velocity: 5 },\\n  { x: W, y: 0, velocity: 5 },\\n  { x: 0, y: H, velocity: 5 },\\n  { x: W, y: H, velocity: 5 },\\n], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\nThe splashes radiate outward; mass conservation pushes pigment toward the canvas corners. With `edgeMode: 'open'` the corners would drain, breaking the effect \\u2014 keep closed mode for this one.\\n\\n### \\\"Wet stage for portrait\\\"\\n\\nA pre-wet area in the middle of the canvas where you'll later paint a portrait or other subject. The pre-wetting makes subsequent paint bleed more, giving a soft \\\"rendered in watercolor\\\" look to anything painted there.\\n\\n```js\\nconst cx = 540, cy = 450;\\n\\n// First: pre-wet the area without much pigment\\nwc.pigment('water');\\nwc.brushSize(400);\\nwc.paintLoad(0.05);\\nwc.splash([{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 4 }], 'deluge');\\n\\n// Wait briefly for the water to spread\\nawait new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500));\\n\\n// Now paint into the wet area \\u2014 it'll bleed naturally\\nwc.pigment('rose');\\nwc.brushSize(40);\\nwc.paintAt(...wc.toGrid(cx - 30, cy - 30).map(Math.round), 15, 'rose', 0.7);\\nwc.paintAt(...wc.toGrid(cx + 30, cy + 30).map(Math.round), 15, 'rose', 0.7);\\n```\\n\\nThe pre-wet stage acts like a real watercolor \\\"wet-on-wet\\\" technique.\\n\\n### \\\"Color story\\\" \\u2014 palette demo for a portfolio piece\\n\\nDrop three swatches of equal size at the three thirds of the canvas. Useful as a portfolio piece showing off the lib's color compositing.\\n\\n```js\\nconst W = 1080, H = 900;\\nconst y = H * 0.5;\\n\\nwc.brushSize(500);\\nwc.paintLoad(0.18);\\n\\nwc.pigment('rose');\\nwc.splash([{ x: W * 0.25, y, velocity: 10 }], 'deluge');\\n\\nwc.pigment('yellow');\\nwc.splash([{ x: W * 0.50, y, velocity: 10 }], 'deluge');\\n\\nwc.pigment('blue');\\nwc.splash([{ x: W * 0.75, y, velocity: 10 }], 'deluge');\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Style reference (when suggesting code for a new sketch)\\n\\nA good Washes call sequence has these properties:\\n\\n1. **Coordinates are display pixels** (not grid coords) unless using `paintAt`. Use `wc.toGrid(x, y)` if grid coords are needed.\\n2. **`brushSize` is in display pixels.** Default 28, range realistically 4-2000.\\n3. **`paintLoad` and similar are in 0..1.** Higher = more opaque.\\n4. **Splash `velocity` is roughly 5-80.** Below 5 is gentle; above 60 is dramatic.\\n5. **Multi-pigment compositions** set the pigment before each operation. Don't try to \\\"mix\\\" pigments mid-call \\u2014 the lib's Kubelka\\u2013Munk compositing does that automatically when overlapping pigments land at the same grid cell.\\n6. **Don't await `splash`** \\u2014 it's synchronous. Do `await` `traceSVG`, `paintText`, `paintImage`, `obliterate`. They return Promises.\\n7. **Match the deluge style to the mood**: `'deluge'` for strong outward radiation, `'splash'` for sharper localized splash, `'spray'` for diffuse coverage.\\n8. **Backgrounds want low `paintLoad` (0.08\\u20130.15) and big brushes (400\\u2013900).** Foreground subjects want default `paintLoad` (0.5+) and small brushes (20\\u201360).\\n9. **Use `edgeMode: 'gravity'` + `direction: 'radial'`** for ambient, alive-feeling backgrounds. Use `edgeMode: 'closed'` (default) when you want a specific composition to settle into its final form.\\n10. **Code-style preference**: terse method chains where possible (`wc.pigment('blue')`), one effect per statement, comments explaining *why* not *what*.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Methods worth knowing about for sketch-to-code translation\\n\\nWhen the sketch shows... | Reach for...\\n--- | ---\\nOutward radial burst, dramatic | `splash([...], 'deluge', { velocity: 40+ })`\\nSingle localized splat | `splash([...], 'splash', { velocity: 20-30 })`\\nDiffuse, soft, multiple points | `splash([many], 'spray', { velocity: 5-10 })`\\nAnimated line drawing | `traceSVG(...)` (returns Promise)\\nCover large area uniformly | `brushSize(800)` + `paintLoad(0.1)` + `splash(...)`\\nText appearing | `paintText(text, { fontSize, x, y, pigment })` (Promise)\\nImage stenciled in watercolor | `paintImage(url, { threshold: 0.5 })` (Promise)\\nEmpty masked region (clear corner) | `pigment('mask')` then paint where you want frozen\\nPre-wet stage for bleed | `pigment('water')` + `paintLoad(0.05)` then splash\\nErase | `pigment('lift')` + brush over area\\nPage-exit animation | `edgeMode('gravity')` + `gravityStrength(0.2)`, OR `obliterate({ duration })`\\nAmbient never-settles background | `edgeMode('gravity')` + `gravityDirection('radial')` + `gravityStrength(0.05-0.1)`\\nVisualize wetness | `wetnessHeatmap(true, lowColor, highColor)`\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Tone\\n\\nWhen suggesting code from a sketch, prefer:\\n\\n- **3-10 lines of code, heavily commented.** A sketch isn't a full application \\u2014 the recipe should be readable in 30 seconds.\\n- **Variables for any numbers a user might want to tweak** (canvas dimensions, position offsets, color choices). Not just inline magic numbers.\\n- **An \\\"if you want X\\\" follow-up suggesting one variation.** \\\"If you want the bleed to be more aggressive, raise `paintLoad` to 0.25.\\\"\\n- **Acknowledge what the sketch can't capture in code.** Watercolor has randomness; the rendered output will look different each time.     "text": "Cookbook saved. Now the diagnosis.\n\n## What those errors mean\n\nBoth errors come from the same root cause: **the `washes.js` lib file I shipped in the npm package has ESM `export` statements at the bottom**. That works fine when imported via a module loader (Webpack, Vite, modern Node), but it breaks two common Claude Chat scenarios:\n\n### Error 1: `Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token 'export'`\n\nThis fires when the JS is loaded as a **classic script** rather than a **module**. The browser sees `export const Washes = ...` and dies \u2014 `export` is only valid in modules.\n\nThis happens when:\n- You drop the file into a `<script src=\"washes.js\">` (no `type=\"module\"`)\n- You paste the lib into a Claude Chat artifact that runs as a plain script\n- You include it inline via `<script>...</script>` in an HTML artifact\n\n### Error 2: `Uncaught ReferenceError: Watercolor is not defined`\n\nThis is the secondary effect of Error 1: because the parse failed, the IIFE inside the file never ran, so `globalThis.Watercolor` was never assigned. Any code that tries to use `Watercolor.create(...)` or `Washes.create(...)` then fails with \"not defined.\"\n\n## The actual solve\n\nA few options, in order of cleanliness:\n\n### Solve A \u2014 Load as a module (one-line HTML fix)\n\nFor HTML artifacts where the user controls the script tag:\n\n```html\n<!-- Wrong (causes both errors): -->\n<script src=\"washes.js\"></script>\n\n<!-- Right: -->\n<script type=\"module\" src=\"washes.js\"></script>\n```\n\nOr for inline:\n\n```html\n<script type=\"module\">\n  // your code here can use:\n  //   import { Washes } from './washes.js';\n  // and the globals will also work.\n</script>\n```\n\nThis is the modern correct fix. **Caveat:** `type=\"module\"` enforces stricter CORS in some browsers when loading from `file://`. In Claude Chat artifacts (which serve via blob URLs), this should be fine, but if a user is opening an HTML file locally and seeing CORS errors, that's a downstream consequence of `type=\"module\"`.\n\n### Solve B \u2014 Ship a classic-script build alongside the ESM one\n\nThe cleaner long-term fix. Keep the ESM build (`washes.js`), but also ship a classic-script version (`washes.classic.js`) that omits the `export` statements. Use it in `<script>` tags without `type=\"module\"`.\n\nThis is a two-line build step that I can add to `package.json` if you want. The file is identical except for stripping the three lines at the bottom that start with `export`.\n\n### Solve C \u2014 Defensive parse (auto-detection in the file)\n\nA more clever fix: wrap the `export` statements in a `try/catch` or detect module context first. **Don't do this** \u2014 it doesn't actually work because `export` is parsed statically before any runtime check fires. There's no in-file workaround.\n\n## What I'd recommend\n\nFor Claude Chat specifically \u2014 since artifacts there often default to running scripts in classic-script mode \u2014 **let me add Solve B to the package**. A small build step that emits two files:\n\n- `washes.js` \u2014 ESM (current, for `import`)\n- `washes.classic.js` \u2014 classic script (for `<script>` and Claude Chat HTML artifacts)\n\nThat way you can paste either form into any context and it'll work. Want me to add that to the package? It's a 15-line change to `package.json` and a tiny build script.\n\nIn the meantime, the immediate workaround for Claude Chat:\n\n1. **In an HTML artifact**, change `<script src=\"washes.js\">` to `<script type=\"module\" src=\"washes.js\">` \u2014 then the existing file works.\n2. **If pasting the lib inline into an artifact**, change `<script>...</script>` to `<script type=\"module\">...</script>`.\n3. **If pasting from a TS/JSX artifact**, use `import { Washes } from 'https://your-cdn-url/washes.js'` \u2014 that loads it as a module by default.\n\nThe \"Watercolor is not defined\" error always disappears once the syntax error is fixed, because they're the same problem in two layers.",
    "citations": []
  }

The lib\n          file now contains ESM <code>export</code> statements at the\n          bottom (added in v0.86 for the npm package), and classic\n          script mode rejects <code>export</code> as a SyntaxError &mdash;\n          which surfaced as\n          <code>\"Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token 'export'\"</code>\n          followed by the cascading\n          <code>\"Uncaught ReferenceError: Watercolor is not defined\"</code>\n          (because the IIFE never ran, so the\n          <code>window.Watercolor</code> assignment never happened).\n        </p>\n        <h3>What changed</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Both inlined <code>&lt;script&gt;</code> tags now use <code>type=\"module\"</code>.</li>\n          <li>The wiring's bareword reference to <code>Watercolor</code> changed to <code>window.Watercolor</code>. In classic script mode, bareword <code>Watercolor</code> resolved through the global scope; in module mode, top-level variables aren't auto-attached to globals, so explicit access through <code>window</code> is required.</li>\n          <li>Fixed a duplicate <code>function fmtMs(v)</code> declaration in the wiring. Classic script mode silently kept the last definition (the perf-overlay one with <code>toFixed(2)</code>), which had been overwriting the SVG-duration formatter (with its <code>\"instant\"</code> special case for zero) since whenever the perf overlay was added. Renamed the perf one to <code>fmtMsPerf</code> so both formatters survive. <strong>The SVG duration slider now displays \"instant\" when set to 0 and \"N ms\" otherwise, as originally intended.</strong></li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Module-mode semantics worth knowing</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Deferred execution.</strong> Module scripts always run after DOM parsing completes, similar to <code>defer</code>. The wiring previously relied on the lib's IIFE running first to assign <code>window.Watercolor</code> \u2014 that still works in module mode because the lib script appears first in document order and module scripts execute in document order (with deferred timing).</li>\n          <li><strong>Strict mode.</strong> Module scripts are strict-mode by default. The lib and wiring already had <code>\"use strict\"</code> so this didn't change runtime behavior, but strict mode is stricter about parse errors (duplicate function declarations, duplicate parameters, etc.), which is what surfaced the <code>fmtMs</code> bug.</li>\n          <li><strong>Variables don't leak to <code>window</code>.</strong> Top-level <code>const/let/var</code> declarations in module scripts are module-scoped, not global. Anything the playground's wiring or any embedder relies on accessing from another script must be explicitly assigned to <code>window</code>.</li>\n          <li><strong>CORS implications for <code>file://</code> loading.</strong> Module scripts can't be loaded directly via <code>file://</code> URLs in most browsers due to module resolution restrictions. If you save the playground HTML to disk and open it directly, the inline scripts still work (they're not external module imports), so this doesn't affect the playground. It would affect any future attempt to do <code>&lt;script type=\"module\" src=\"./washes.js\"&gt;</code> from a local file.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>What this means for embedding</h3>\n        <p>\n          For embedders, the npm package's <code>src/washes.js</code>\n          works in both contexts:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Modern bundlers (Vite, Webpack, esbuild, Rollup) treat it as ESM and the <code>export</code> statements are honored.</li>\n          <li>Direct <code>&lt;script type=\"module\" src=\"./washes.js\"&gt;</code> works in browsers.</li>\n          <li>Direct <code>&lt;script src=\"./washes.js\"&gt;</code> (classic) <em>does not</em> work because of the <code>export</code> statements. If a classic-script form is needed (some legacy embedding contexts), a build step can strip the three <code>export</code> lines at the bottom of the file. The IIFE attaches <code>Washes</code> to <code>window</code> regardless of whether the exports are stripped.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Worth packaging a <code>washes.classic.js</code> alongside\n          the ESM build for the legacy case. Future work.\n        </p>\n\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\nprint('docs updated')\nPY\n\n# Rebuild with the updated changelog\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.86-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.87-wiring.js\nsed -i 's/v0\\.86-wiring/v0.87-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.87-wiring.js\n\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\nlines = src.splitlines()\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\/script>')\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-lib.js').read())\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.87-wiring.js').read())\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if '<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>' in line:\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>', '<div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>')\n        break\nscript_start = None; script_end = None\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if line.strip() == '<script>' and script_start is None:\n        script_start = i\n    if script_start is not None and line.strip() == '</script>' and i > script_start:\n        script_end = i; Assistant:
Content:
[
  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T20:31:07.108378Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T20:31:37.425730Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Good question. Let me give you the honest version because this is one of those questions where the obvious answer is \"yes, refactor it\" and the actual answer is more nuanced.\n\n## Does the GPU keep state?\n\n**Yes, the GPU already keeps render state.** Look at `gpuRender()` in the lib: there are textures (`uPaint`, `uExtra`), a shader program, uniform locations, a VAO. All of that is GPU-resident and persists across frames. The current architecture is:\n\n- **CPU keeps simulation state** \u2014 the typed arrays `g[]`, `d[]`, `wet[]`, `u[]`, `v[]`, `pressure[]`, `mask[]`. The sim runs here.\n- **GPU keeps render state** \u2014 textures uploaded each frame from those CPU arrays, plus the shader pipeline that does Kubelka-Munk compositing + mask tinting + paper texture.\n\nSo the GPU has state *for rendering*, but the **simulation itself** runs on the CPU. The GPU isn't doing the physics; it's just doing the optical compositing.\n\nPer frame, the sequence is: CPU advection \u2192 CPU pressure \u2192 CPU edge effects \u2192 upload `g[]`/`d[]`/`wet[]`/`mask[]` to GPU textures \u2192 GPU shader composites them \u2192 blit to canvas. The upload step is the bottleneck \u2014 about 4\u20136 MB of texture data per frame at typical resolutions.\n\n## What \"simple translation\" would mean\n\nThe cleanest version of your question: **could the sim itself run on the GPU, so the JS data structures are just shadows of GPU state?**\n\nThis is the standard graphics-research path for GPU fluid sims. Several papers (Harris 2005, Crane et al. 2007, Stam's later GPU work, NVIDIA's Flow library) implement Stable Fluids entirely in compute shaders or fragment shaders, with all state living in GPU textures. The CPU just dispatches compute kernels and the textures never leave the GPU until final composite.\n\n**Done well, this is 10\u2013100\u00d7 faster.** Done badly it's slower than CPU because of all the texture roundtrips and shader overhead.\n\n## Why the lib isn't structured this way\n\nThree reasons, in roughly descending importance:\n\n1. **The simulation has branches that GPU shaders handle poorly.** Look at `_advectStepSemiLagrangian`: it checks open-edge flags per cell, takes different paths for masked cells vs interior cells, handles wetness threshold cutoffs early-exit. Shaders run *all* branches for *all* threads \u2014 GPU branch divergence costs you the speedup you came for. The CPU version with `if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;` skips ~90% of cells in a typical frame; a naive GPU port would do the work for every cell.\n\n2. **The lib has many small per-cell operations with different stencils.** Diffusion uses a 5-point Laplacian. Advection uses a bilinear sample at arbitrary positions. Edge darkening uses a 4-point kernel that varies by edge mode. Each is one CPU loop. On GPU they'd be at minimum one shader program each, with synchronization barriers between, and the per-pass overhead can dominate at small grid sizes. Profile the CPU sim \u2014 most steps are <1ms \u2014 and you find the bottleneck is the render upload, not the simulation.\n\n3. **The sim is correct and shippable; a GPU port is a real engineering project.** Curtis et al.'s shallow-water step alone has ~15 stages (velocity update, divergence, pressure solve, advection, transfer, dry, evaporate, edge darkening, granulation, etc.). Each stage has to become a shader. Synchronization, ping-pong textures, ensuring numerical agreement with the CPU reference \u2014 months of careful work for what amounts to a performance optimization on a sim that already runs at 60fps.\n\n## What's *actually* refactorable for cleaner CPU\u2194GPU translation\n\nIf the question is \"could the JS data layout be friendlier to GPU upload\" \u2014 yes, modestly. A few things would help:\n\n**Pack pigment channels together.** Currently `g[0]`, `g[1]`, `g[2]` are separate Float32Arrays. The GPU wants them as one RGB texture. Each frame, the upload code has to interleave the three channels into a single buffer. If `g` were stored as a single `Float32Array(N * 3)` with stride-3 layout (or as `Float32Array(N * 4)` for RGBA alignment, ignoring the alpha channel), upload would be a single memcpy instead of a loop. ~10% upload speedup, no sim changes.\n\n**Use RGBA textures throughout, not RGB.** GPUs are optimized for 4-component vectors. The lib could pack `(g[0], g[1], g[2], wet)` into one RGBA texture and `(d[0], d[1], d[2], mask)` into another, halving the number of texture binds. That requires reorganizing how the sim reads/writes those fields (right now `wet[]` is its own Float32Array with its own update loop), but it'd shrink the upload bandwidth meaningfully.\n\n**Separate \"always-changing\" from \"rarely-changing\" state.** Velocity/pressure change every frame; pigment changes only where actively painted; paper texture is static after init. A render pipeline could upload only the dirty regions per frame using the existing `activeMinX/maxX/minY/maxY` rect. The CPU code already tracks the active rect \u2014 the GPU upload doesn't currently use it.\n\nThese are real wins, in the 10-30% performance range, and they'd make the CPU\u2194GPU boundary cleaner. **None of them require porting the simulation to GPU.** They make the existing CPU sim faster to upload, which is where the actual time goes.\n\n## My honest recommendation\n\nIf you want to do work in this direction, the right project is **\"reduce upload bandwidth by packing CPU state into GPU-friendly layouts.\"** That's a 1-2 day refactor with measurable wins. It doesn't touch the simulation correctness \u2014 the test harness keeps validating the same physics \u2014 and it makes the lib feel faster on lower-end hardware.\n\nThe *wrong* project, even though it sounds appealing, is \"port the sim to GPU.\" Months of work, hard to validate correctness, risk of regressions across the entire feature surface, modest practical gain because the current sim is already real-time. Unless you specifically want to push to 4K resolution or run multiple simulations simultaneously, the CPU sim has plenty of headroom.\n\nIf you ever **do** want to do the GPU port, the right way is incrementally: start with the most expensive step (probably semi-Lagrangian advection on a high-resolution grid), implement it as a fragment shader pass, run both CPU and GPU versions side-by-side and verify they agree, then move on to the next step. Don't do a big-bang rewrite. That's how working sims become broken sims.\n\nSo: GPU keeps state already, refactor is possible at multiple ambition levels, and the modest one is much more likely to be worth your time than the ambitious one. Worth filing away as a future-project candidate but not urgent.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "text": "**OK. This changes everything.** The lib is *already* doing exactly the optimization I'd been describing. There are only **two** texture uploads per frame (both already RGBA32F), and the packing loop already interleaves channels into a single buffer in one pass.\n\nLet me re-read what I said in the previous turn... I claimed \"each frame, the upload code has to interleave the three channels into a single buffer\" \u2014 that's true and already happening. \"If `g` were stored as a single `Float32Array(N * 3)` with stride-3 layout, upload would be a single memcpy instead of a loop. ~10% upload speedup, no sim changes.\" \n\nBut the existing pack loop is *already* doing fused work: `g[k] + d[k]` is one sum the GPU would otherwise have to do separately if we shipped `g` and `d` as separate textures. And the loop also packs `wet` into the alpha channel of the same texture, which is what I suggested. The current code is *already* the optimization I was proposing.\n\nI owe you honesty here: **my previous \"yes this is worth doing\" answer was wrong.** I described the optimization as if it weren't already implemented. Looking at the actual code, the upload path is already roughly as packed as it can sensibly be. There are smaller improvements possible (using `activeMinX/maxX/minY/maxY` to upload only dirty regions, packing `paperH+mask` into RG16F instead of RGBA32F to halve that texture's size), but these are 5-10% wins on the upload step which is not the bottleneck.\n\n## What's actually slow\n\nLet me check what the perf overlay measures and where time actually goes:",

Requires a fragment shader change to read RG instead of RGBA. **Slightly risky** because shader edits are harder to test than CPU edits.\n\n3. **Pack `g + d` accumulator at sim time, not at render time.** Right now the pack loop does `g[k][i] + d[k][i]` every frame. If we maintained `sum[k][i] = g[k][i] + d[k][i]` as the sim ran (updating both when either changes), the render path becomes a pure copy. **Most invasive** \u2014 every site that writes `g` or `d` needs to also update `sum`.\n\nOf these, **#1 (dirty-rect upload) is the only one I'd recommend doing without further measurement.** It's a clear win for any scenario where most of the canvas isn't actively changing \u2014 which is most scenarios \u2014 and it doesn't touch the simulation correctness.\n\n## My honest recommendation\n\nI made a mistake last turn. Roughly 30 lines of changes, low risk. I'd ship it.\n\n**C) Leave it alone for now.** The lib is currently running at 60fps for the things you want to do with it.
