    "type": "text",
    "text": " Honest answer: **partially possible, with important caveats about what \"replicate\" can actually mean.**\n\nLet me think through what's achievable in Node and what isn't, because the gap matters.\n\n## What's straightforward to simulate\n\n**Grid size and resolution.** The lib's `SCALE` option controls cells per display pixel. Lower-end devices typically run at higher SCALE (fewer cells, faster). I can run the sim at scales matching common device profiles \u2014 a Pixel 6 might use SCALE=3, a MacBook Pro might use SCALE=1.5 \u2014 and measure how long each `simStep()` takes.\n\n**Workload patterns.** I can construct scenarios matching real use: idle paper (no active cells), single brush stroke (small active rect), big deluge (entire canvas active), radial gravity + ambient flow (everything always active). These translate directly to sim time.\n\n**Pure JS execution speed.** Node uses V8, same engine as Chrome. CPU-bound JS code runs at very similar speeds in Node and Chrome on the same machine. So `simStep()` timings in Node are a reasonable proxy for desktop Chrome timings.\n\n## What's harder or impossible\n\n**GPU rendering.** Node has no WebGL2 by default. The lib's `gpuRender()` won't run at all in Node. I can measure CPU-render time but not GPU-render time. For desktop scenarios where WebGL is active, **Node can't tell you what frame rate you'll actually see** \u2014 only what the CPU sim costs.\n\n**Device-specific CPU performance.** A Pixel 6's CPU is roughly 1/5 to 1/3 the single-threaded JS speed of a modern desktop. I can't make my Node process run that slowly authentically. **The honest workaround is to compute a slowdown multiplier** based on published benchmarks (Speedometer 3, JetStream2) and apply it to the measured times. This gives you \"if this took 8ms here, it would take ~30ms on a Pixel 6\" \u2014 useful as an estimate, not a measurement.\n\n**Mobile-specific behaviors.** Touch event handling, pointer pressure, palm rejection, throttling under thermal load, battery-saving CPU caps. None of these are simulatable in Node. They have to be tested on actual devices.\n\n**Memory pressure.** Mobile devices have tight memory budgets; allocation patterns that work on desktop can trigger GC pauses on phones. Node has plenty of RAM, so memory pressure won't show up in a Node harness.\n\n## What I'd actually build, if you want it\n\nA `washes-perf-harness.cjs` script that:\n\n1. **Defines device profiles** \u2014 desktop-high, desktop-mid, laptop-mid, mobile-mid, mobile-low \u2014 each with `(SCALE, slowdown_multiplier)` based on published benchmarks\n2. **Runs a fixed set of workload scenarios** for each profile \u2014 idle, single splash, deluge, radial gravity, full-canvas paint\n3. **Measures per-step time** using `performance.now()` ranges over many iterations, takes p50/p95/p99\n4. **Applies the slowdown multiplier** to produce projected mobile times\n5. **Flags scenarios that exceed budget** \u2014 e.g., \"this would drop below 30fps on mobile-low\"\n6. **Outputs a markdown table** of results for inclusion in the README or maintainer docs\n\n**What it won't tell you:**\n- Whether the GPU path is fast enough on mobile (need real device)\n- Whether actual touch input feels right (need real device)\n- Whether memory pressure causes hitches (need real device)\n- Whether thermal throttling kicks in during long sessions (need real device, sustained load)\n\nThe harness would be a useful **regression test** \u2014 \"did my last change make sim 20% slower?\" \u2014 and a useful **early warning** \u2014 \"this new feature pushes mobile-low above frame budget\" \u2014 but it's NOT a substitute for testing on real devices when you're close to shipping.\n\n## A subtler question worth surfacing\n\nThe lib has features whose performance depends on configuration in ways that won't show up in canned workloads:\n\n- **Brush size at 2000px** stamps over essentially the whole grid in one frame\n- **Open boundaries with high gravity strength** keeps every wet cell active forever\n- **Wetness heatmap** adds a per-frame full-canvas pass on top of the sim\n- **Multiple simultaneous features** (e.g., gravity + heatmap + WebGL fallback to CPU) compound\n\nSo the harness needs to test **combinations**, not just isolated features. That's what would have actually caught real performance regressions.\n\n## Two ways to proceed\n\n**Option A: Build the harness, with honest reporting.** I write the script, define ~5 device profiles with citation-backed slowdown multipliers, run ~8 workload scenarios per profile, output a markdown table. ~1 hour of work. The README gets a \"performance profile\" section with current measurements. You get a regression-detection tool.\n\n**Option B: Skip the simulated harness, build a tiny in-browser perf page instead.** A separate `examples/perf.html` that loads the lib, runs the same workload scenarios, but does so in a real browser where you can test it on actual devices via Chrome DevTools device emulation, BrowserStack, or your own phone. Gives you *real* numbers from real GPUs and real mobile CPUs. ~2 hours of work but the numbers are trustworthy.\n\n**Option C: Do both.** Node harness for fast CI-style regression detection (catches \"I made the sim 30% slower\"); browser harness for periodic device-specific validation (catches \"this drops below 30fps on iPhone SE\"). Different tools for different questions.\n\nMy honest recommendation: **B over A.** A Node harness with synthetic multipliers gives you confidence-looking numbers that don't actually predict real-world performance well. A browser perf page tested on real devices is slower to set up but tells you the truth. If you want both, do B first, then add A later as a CI-friendly regression check.\n\nWant me to do B? Or A? Or C? Or hold off entirely until you have a specific performance question to answer?",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Smart move. You're asking for **closed-edge physics + drained-edge appearance** \u2014 pigment is still there mathematically (mass conserved, no edge effects from the boundary work), but it's hidden from the rendered output so it looks like it drained.\n\nLet me think through what this means before coding.\n\n## What the flag should do\n\nThe pigment accumulates in real cells near the boundary because closed mode reflects flow back. Two ways to \"hide\" it:\n\n**Option A \u2014 Alpha fade-out at the render layer.** In the last N pixels at each edge, multiply the rendered alpha by a function that goes from 1.0 at distance N to 0.0 at the boundary. The pigment is still there in the simulation arrays, but the render pass makes it transparent at the edges. Mass is conserved exactly; the canvas just looks like it bleeds off.\n\n**Option B \u2014 Hard pigment removal at the render layer.** In the last N pixels at each edge, override pigment to paper color in the render output. Sharper visual cutoff. Possibly looks too clean.\n\n**Option C \u2014 Both: alpha fade-out, with the fade depth user-configurable.** A slider sets how many pixels the fade region is. Default depth somewhere in the 20-40 pixel range so the falloff is visible but not so wide it eats into the composition.\n\nI'd go with **A** with a fixed sensible default (say 24 pixels) and a user setting only if you want to tune it later. Keeps the UI uncluttered.\n\n## What it doesn't address\n\nThe performance question you asked about \u2014 gravity + closed edges. The gravity bias currently *only fires* in `'gravity'` mode (per the v0.82 fix). In closed mode, the bias is ignored. So \"gravity + closed edges\" as currently implemented does nothing extra. To get the visual effect of gravity *and* the performance of closed-edge physics, we'd need to:\n\n1. **Allow the gravity bias to apply in closed mode** (decoupling it from edge state), OR\n2. **Add a new combined mode** like `'closed-fade'` that's closed physics + render fade\n\nThe cleaner answer is **#1**: let `gravityStrength` apply regardless of edge mode, gated by the value itself (zero = no bias, any positive value = active). Then \"closed edges with gravity + fade rendering\" becomes a meaningful configuration.\n\n## Performance question, honest answer\n\nClosed edges *are* slightly faster than open/gravity because `drainBoundaries()` is a no-op when nothing's open. But it's not a huge win \u2014 the drainage pass only touches 4 \u00d7 (GW or GH) cells per frame, which is a few thousand cells out of ~350,000 total. The dominant cost is the interior advection + diffusion, which is the same in all modes.\n\nThe bigger performance question is whether the **gravity bias itself** is slower. It's one extra add per cell in the velocity update \u2014 negligible. The thing that *might* be slower in gravity mode is that gravity keeps the field \"alive\" forever (pigment never fully settles), so the `wet < 0.04` early-exit fires for fewer cells. That's the real cost, and it's specific to gravity-mode dynamics, not edge mode.\n\nSo: **closed edges + gravity bias** should be slightly cheaper than **gravity edges + gravity bias**, but probably by ~5-10%, not a transformative win. The render fade is the visual feature; the perf improvement is a side benefit.\n\n## What I'd actually build\n\nThree coupled changes:\n\n1. **Decouple gravity bias from edge mode.** Apply it whenever `gravityStrength > 0`, regardless of `edgeMode`. The flag I'd add: `gravityBiasInClosedMode` or similar \u2014 but actually simpler to just always apply the bias when strength > 0. **Backward compat:** with `gravityStrength = 0` (the default), nothing changes for existing closed-mode users.\n\n2. **Add edge fade rendering option.** New API `wc.edgeFade(pixels)` \u2014 pass a positive number to enable, zero to disable. Default `0` (off). When enabled, the CPU + GPU render paths apply an alpha falloff in the last N pixels at each edge.\n\n3. **Add UI control.** A \"Edge fade\" slider, range 0-80 pixels, default 0 (off).\n\nBefore I build, a few decisions:\n\n**Q1: Should the edge fade always be available, or only when edges are closed?** \n- If always available, it composes \u2014 you could have open edges *plus* fade rendering for an even softer effect.\n- If only-in-closed, it's a clear \"the fade is hiding pigment that's actually there\" mode.\n\nI'd vote always available \u2014 gives you more compositional options.\n\n**Q2: Should the gravity bias really apply in closed mode by default, or only when explicitly requested?**\n\nCurrently `gravityStrength` only fires in gravity mode. Changing that means anyone who's set `gravityStrength` for use with gravity mode would suddenly see it in closed mode too. **No one is currently doing this** (the lib hasn't shipped with that behavior), but it does change semantics. \n\nTwo options:\n- **Apply gravity bias whenever strength > 0** (simplest, requires user to set strength to 0 when switching back to pure closed)\n- **Apply gravity bias when strength > 0 AND edge mode is gravity OR a new \"closedWithGravity\" mode** (more explicit, no surprise)\n\nI lean toward the first because it's simpler, but it does change `closed + gravityStrength > 0` from no-op to active-bias. Wanted to flag it.\n\n**Q3: Fade falloff shape \u2014 linear or smooth?**\n- Linear: `alpha *= distance_from_edge / fade_depth`. Sharp gradient.\n- Smoothstep: `alpha *= smoothstep(0, fade_depth, distance_from_edge)`. Soft S-curve, more natural-looking watercolor edge.\n\nI'd go with smoothstep. Looks more like real wet-paper bleed.\n\nTell me if my answers to Q1/Q2/Q3 are right, and I'll build it. Or override any of them and I'll adjust.",
    "citations": []
  }

Slider 0..1 of the\n  // public API maps directly here.\n  _gravityBiasX = _gravityStrength * VEL_CLAMP * dx;\n  _gravityBiasY = _gravityStrength * VEL_CLAMP * dy;\n  if (_edgeMode === 'closed') {\n    _edgeOpenLeft = _edgeOpenRight = _edgeOpenTop = _edgeOpenBottom = false;\n    // Bias still recorded for when mode flips back, but only applied\n    // in updateVelocity if at least one edge is open. (Pure bias with\n    // closed edges would just pile mass at the downwind edge \u2014 not\n    // useful, and currently undocumented behavior so safer to gate.)\n  } else if (_edgeMode === 'open') {\n    _edgeOpenLeft = _edgeOpenRight = _edgeOpenTop = _edgeOpenBottom = true;\n  } else if (_edgeMode === 'gravity') {\n    // v0.83 \u2014 'radial' opens all four edges since the pull is\n    // omnidirectional. Cardinal/diagonal directions still open\n    // only the edge(s) facing the pull.\n    if (_gravityDir === 'radial') {\n      _edgeOpenLeft = _edgeOpenRight = _edgeOpenTop = _edgeOpenBottom = true;\n    } else {\n      // Open edges in the direction of gravity. A diagonal opens two\n      // edges; a cardinal opens just one. Use sign of dx/dy with a\n      // small epsilon to handle floating-point exact zeros.\n      _edgeOpenRight  = dx >  0.001;\n      _edgeOpenLeft   = dx < -0.001;\n      _edgeOpenBottom = dy >  0.001;\n      _edgeOpenTop    = dy < -0.001;\n    }\n  }\n}\n// mask[] and maskActive declared after N is initialized \u2014 see the\n// state-arrays block below.\n// Shared output array to avoid per-call allocation. Gravity controls are now enabled in BOTH 'gravity' and\n  // 'closed-gravity' modes (both apply the bias). Closed and Open\n  // still gray them out.\n  const labels = {\n    'closed':         'Edges: Closed',\n    'closed-gravity': 'Edges: Closed+Gravity',\n    'open':           'Edges: Open',\n    'gravity':        'Edges: Gravity',\n  };\n  edgesBtn.innerHTML = (labels[mode] || labels.closed) +\n    '<span class=\"info-marker\" aria-hidden=\"true\">\u24d8</span>';\n  const on = mode !== 'closed';\n  edgesBtn.classList.toggle('on', on);\n  edgesBtn.setAttribute('aria-pressed', String(on));\n  const gravityActive = mode === 'gravity' || mode === 'closed-gravity';\n  gravDir.disabled = !gravityActive;\n  gravStr.disabled = !gravityActive;\n  gravDir.style.opacity = gravityActive ? Closed-edge physics (no\n          drainage, mass conserved) but gravity bias IS applied.\n          Pigment drifts in the gravity direction and piles up at\n          the downwind edge. Compared to <code>'gravity'</code>\n          mode: same dynamics inside the canvas, but no pigment\n          leaves through the edges.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Numerical test (centered splash, downward gravity at\n          Pull=0.10, 100 simSteps):\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">closed         | mass: 174960 \u2192 166012  | centroid Y drift: -0.04  (no gravity)\nclosed-gravity | mass: 174960 \u2192 170429  | centroid Y drift: +9.08  (gravity, no drain)\ngravity        | mass: 174960 \u2192 170432  | centroid Y drift: ~ same  (gravity + drain)</pre>\n        <p>\n          The centroid drift confirms the gravity bias is doing its\n          job; the mass-loss number stays in the \"evaporation only\"\n          range because no edges are open.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>New render option: edge fade</h3>\n        <p>\n          Render-time alpha falloff in the last N grid cells at each\n          edge, using a smoothstep curve. The pigment is still\n          mathematically present at the edge cells; the render just\n          makes it transparent. Independent of edge mode &mdash;\n          composes freely with any of <code>closed</code> /\n          <code>closed-gravity</code> / <code>open</code> /\n          <code>gravity</code>.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Primary use case: pair with <code>closed-gravity</code>\n          to get gravity dynamics without the visible edge buildup\n          that closed-mode would otherwise show. The result is\n          gravity behavior with the visual of pigment falling off\n          the page &mdash; but without the actual mass-leaving\n          physics that <code>gravity</code> mode requires.\n        </p>\n        <h3>API</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.edgeMode('closed-gravity')   // new mode value\nwc.edgeFade(24)                  // 24-cell smoothstep falloff at each edge\nwc.edgeFade(0)                   // disable\nwc.edgeFade()                    // read current value</pre>\n        <p>\n          Both CPU and WebGL render paths implement the fade.\n          GLSL <code>smoothstep()</code> matches the CPU\n          <code>t*t*(3-2t)</code> form to within float precision.\n          Per-axis fractions are passed as a <code>vec2</code>\n          uniform so non-square canvases get geometrically correct\n          falloff at both axes.\n        </p>\n        <h3>UI changes</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>The Edges button now cycles through four states: Closed \u2192 Closed+Gravity \u2192 Open \u2192 Gravity \u2192 Closed.</li>\n          <li>Gravity direction dropdown and Pull slider are now enabled in BOTH gravity modes (was previously only enabled in <code>gravity</code>).</li>\n          <li>New \"Edge fade\" slider just below Pull. Range 0-80 pixels, default 0 (off). Two related features:\n\n**Edges button now cycles through four modes:** Closed \u2192 **Closed+Gravity** \u2192 Open \u2192 Gravity \u2192 Closed.\n\nIn `Closed+Gravity`, you get the gravity bias and dynamics with no actual drainage. Implemented in both CPU and GPU render paths.\n\n**The combination you wanted:** Edges set to `Closed+Gravity`, gravity direction `Down` (or whatever), Pull 10-20, Edge fade 24-40 pixels. You should see pigment drift in the gravity direction, with the buildup invisibly faded away at the bottom edge. Mass is fully conserved (good for long ambient backgrounds that shouldn't slowly evaporate everything).\n\n**Performance honest note:** I claimed `closed-gravity` would be slightly faster than `gravity` because `drainBoundaries()` is a no-op. That's true \u2014 but the saving is small (a few thousand cell operations out of ~350k per frame). The real motivation for this feature is visual: getting the gravity look without the edge-drainage artifacts. If you specifically want a perf win, this isn't where you'll see it. If you want the visual effect of *gravity that doesn't lose mass*, this nails it.\n\nTry `Closed+Gravity` + `Radial` direction + Edge fade 40. That might be a particularly nice ambient background \u2014 pigment continuously drifts outward, edges fade smoothly, nothing ever actually leaves the canvas so it can run indefinitely.",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-14T04:04:31.630966Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-14T04:05:17.879909Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Good question, and one worth thinking carefully about before suggesting code. There are several distinct sub-questions hiding inside it:\n\n1. **How do you detect that you're on a low-tier or throttled device?**\n2. **How aggressively should you adapt, and when?**\n3. **What knobs are worth adapting?**\n4. **What's the failure mode if you guess wrong?**\n\nLet me think through each.\n\n## Device detection \u2014 what's actually available\n\nThe honest answer is: **detecting hardware tier from the browser is mostly bad and you should rely on it less than you'd think.**\n\nA few signals exist:\n\n- **`navigator.hardwareConcurrency`** \u2014 CPU thread count. Modern phones often report 6-8 (same as low-end laptops), so this is a weak signal. M-series Macs and budget Android both report 8.\n- **`navigator.deviceMemory`** \u2014 GB of RAM, rounded down. Reports `4` for any phone with 4-8GB. Available in Chrome and Edge but not Firefox or Safari.\n- **`matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')`** \u2014 touch-primary device. Loosely correlates with mobile, but a tablet with a stylus has both fine and coarse pointers.\n- **`navigator.userAgent`** \u2014 string parsing. Brittle and increasingly meaningless as browsers reduce UA fingerprinting.\n- **`navigator.connection.effectiveType`** \u2014 4g/3g/2g/slow-2g. Signals network, not compute. A phone on wifi reports \"4g\" regardless of how slow the CPU is.\n- **`navigator.userAgentData.mobile`** \u2014 Chrome only. True on mobile.\n\nNone of these tell you \"this device is throttled right now.\" They tell you static facts about the device, not its current state.\n\n## The real signal: measure, don't predict\n\n**The thing that actually works is measuring frame time and adapting.** A high-end phone in a hot car is slower than a low-end phone in air conditioning; static signals miss that entirely. A user with a lot of other tabs open is slower than a clean profile. Thermal throttling, background sync, battery saver mode \u2014 none of these show up in device detection but all of them show up in frame time.\n\nThe lib *already* has the data: `wc.perfMetrics()` returns `framep50`, `framep95`, `framep99`. The right pattern is:\n\n1. **Run normally for the first few seconds.** Give the user the full experience.\n2. **Watch frame time.** If p95 stays under your budget, do nothing.\n3. **If p95 exceeds budget for a sustained window**, step down to a lighter configuration.\n4. **Stop adjusting once you find a stable point** (or you'll oscillate).\n\nThis is essentially how YouTube and Netflix adapt video quality \u2014 they measure throughput, they don't predict from hardware specs.\n\n## Static hints \u2014 when they DO help\n\nThere's one case where static hints earn their keep: **the very first frames**. Before you've measured anything, you have to guess at a starting configuration. For that:\n\n- `matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')` is a reasonable proxy for \"start at a lower resolution\" \u2014 most coarse-pointer devices are mobile and benefit from a higher `SCALE`.\n- Beyond that, default high and adapt down.\n\nDon't try to detect \"low-tier mobile\" specifically and start it at the lowest setting. You'll be wrong often enough that high-end mobile users (which is most mobile users now) will get a worse experience than they should.\n\n## What knobs are worth adapting\n\nLooking at the lib, the cost-vs-quality knobs are roughly:\n\n| Knob | Cost reduction | Quality impact |\n|---|---|---|\n| `scale` (up) | Large \u2014 quadratic in grid cell count | Visible \u2014 pigment blocks get bigger |\n| `webgl(true)` | Already on by default when available | None |\n| `cursorPreview(false)` | Tiny | Loses brush-size visualization |\n| `advectionMode` | Modest | semi-Lagrangian \u2192 standard saves ~10% but reintroduces cross artifact |\n| `evaporation` (up) | Modest \u2014 fewer wet cells means fewer active cells | Wash dries faster, less time for bleed |\n| `edgeMode === 'closed'` | Modest \u2014 skips drainage pass | None functional, but loses edge effects |\n| `wetnessHeatmap(false)` | Modest \u2014 skips overlay render | None for users not using it |\n\nThe big one is `scale`. Doubling SCALE quarters the cell count. That's a massive win for compute-bound scenarios and the visual cost is usually acceptable (pigment looks slightly chunkier; most users won't notice).\n\n## A staged-adaptation strategy\n\nThe pattern I'd actually recommend:\n\n**Stage 0 \u2014 initial guess from static signals (one-time, before first frame)**\n\n```js\nconst isCoarsePointer = matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)').matches;\nconst isMobileUA = navigator.userAgentData?.mobile ?? false;\n\nconst initialScale = (isCoarsePointer || isMobileUA) ? 2.5 : 1.75;\n// Other defaults stay as-is. Don't disable features speculatively.\n```\n\nThis is the only static-signal use I'd defend. Mobile starts at a lower resolution; everyone else starts at the lib's default. No feature disabling at this stage.\n\n**Stage 1 \u2014 measure for 3 seconds at the initial config**\n\nDon't react instantly to spikes \u2014 give the JIT time to warm up, give the user a few interactions, and only react to *sustained* slowness.\n\n**Stage 2 \u2014 adapt only on clear, sustained signal**\n\n```js\n// After 3 seconds: check p95 frame time. If it's been bad for the\n// whole window, step down.\nsetInterval(() => {\n  const m = wc.perfMetrics();\n  if (m.framep95 > 33) {  // dropping below 30fps p95\n    // Step down ONE level at a time. Don't hop straight to lowest.\n    if (wc.scale() < 3) {\n      wc.scale(wc.scale() + 0.5);\n    } else if (wc.wetnessHeatmap()) {\n      wc.wetnessHeatmap(false);\n    } else if (wc.cursorPreview()) {\n      wc.cursorPreview(false);\n    }\n    // ... etc\n  }\n}, 5000);\n```\n\n**Stage 3 \u2014 STOP adapting once you find equilibrium**\n\nTrack how many adjustments you've made; cap it. Avoid the failure mode where one bad frame triggers a cascade of downgrades.\n\n## What I'd actually recommend you build\n\n**Don't auto-adapt by default.** I want to argue against the version of this question that's \"make the lib detect everything and adjust on its own.\" Here's why:\n\n- Users with throttled-but-recoverable conditions (a phone that's hot, a tab that just got CPU-starved by another tab) will get permanently degraded UX once the lib adapts down. There's no good way to detect recovery.\n- Auto-adaptation makes the lib's behavior unpredictable from the embedder's perspective. \"Why does it look different on this device?\" \"We adapted!\" \u2014 that's not a story most embedders want.\n- The lib is currently a deterministic tool. Adding adaptation makes it an opinionated tool. That's a real architectural shift, not just a feature.\n\n**Instead, make adaptation an embedder-driven opt-in.** Provide the data and the methods; let the embedder decide whether to use them.\n\nThree concrete things I'd add to the lib (none of them automatic):\n\n1. **A `getPerfTier()` method** that returns a hint based on `framep95` over the last few seconds. Returns `'good' | 'marginal' | 'poor'` or similar. The lib doesn't act on this; it just reports it.\n\n2. **An optional autoAdapt config**, off by default, that turns on the staged-adaptation logic above. Embedders who want it can opt in:\n   ```js\n   Washes.create(host, { autoAdapt: { targetFps: 30, maxScale: 3 } });\n   ```\n\n3. **A `quality` preset method** so embedders can implement their own adaptation cheaply:\n   ```js\n   wc.quality('high');    // scale=1.5, all features\n   wc.quality('medium');  // scale=2, all features\n   wc.quality('low');     // scale=3, heatmap off, cursor preview off\n   wc.quality('minimum'); // scale=4, donor-cell advection, ...\n   ```\n\nThe third one is the most useful in practice. Most embedders don't want to implement frame-time monitoring; they want a knob to expose to their users (\"Quality: High / Medium / Low\") and have it Just Work.\n\n## My actual answer to your question\n\nWhat I'd build, in order of cost-effectiveness:\n\n1. **First \u2014 and maybe only \u2014 `wc.quality(preset)`.** A simple method that bundles a coherent set of settings under named presets. This is something embedders can actually use, and it makes the lib's degradation modes explicit and discoverable. Maybe 30 lines of code.\n\n2. **Second \u2014 `Washes.create(host, { qualityHint: 'auto-mobile' })`** that uses just `matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')` to pick a starting preset. Static-only, no runtime adaptation. Embedders who want \"good default for mobile\" get it. ~5 lines.\n\n3. **Third (only if you want it) \u2014 runtime auto-adapt.** This is real engineering with real failure modes. Not recommended unless you have a specific use case.\n\nIf you want to ship something now, I'd build #1 and #2 only. They're cheap, they're predictable, and they cover most of what \"auto-set for low-tier devices\" practically means without taking on the complexity of runtime adaptation.\n\nWant me to do that, or were you thinking of something different?",
    "citations": []
  }
